notes: All endpoints verified reachable via WebSearch/WebFetch on 2026-07-09. Prioritized official/primary + free. Key duplication flags: Data Mexico trade cubes are ALREADY WIRED (listed only as a "go deeper" note, marked duplicate); World Bank macro is wired but World Bank WITS (tariffs/TRAINS) is a DISTINCT product and not a duplicate; Banxico SIE remittances/FX are wired but INEGI BCMM merchandise-trade series are distinct. Best bilateral Mexico-US volume by HS + by state: US Census International Trade API (US side, monthly, by state of origin/destination + HS) and USITC DataWeb (US side, integrates tariff treatment) are the two workhorses; UN Comtrade gives both-sided mirror data; INEGI BCMM is the Mexican-authority print. Best applied+threatened tariff rates: USITC HTS reststop (the legal US schedule incl. Ch.99 Section 232/301/IEEPA overlays), Federal Register API (the actual proclamations/notices that set threatened + enacted rates, dated, daily), WITS/TRAINS (Mexico's own applied+MFN+preferential schedule), and Mexico SIAVI (Mexican import tariff by fracción + treaty preference). CBP CSMS is the operational "what rate is CBP actually collecting today" feed. Watch-outs: Comtrade free preview caps at 500 records/call (register free key for bulk); Census + DataWeb + BEA all require a free API key; SIAVI has no API (stable scrape of siavi.economia.gob.mx by fracción); CBP CSMS best consumed via its GovDelivery bulletin feed rather than a documented REST API.

sources.name: US Census International Trade API (monthly, HS + by state)
sources.whatItGives: Official US-side monthly merchandise imports and exports with the fullest granularity available: by HS commodity code, by country (Mexico = 2010), by US state of origin/destination, by port, and by district. This is the primary source for bilateral Mexico-US trade VOLUME broken down by product and by state that Alan flagged.
keyIndicators: US imports from Mexico (value, monthly, by HS2-HS10)
US exports to Mexico (value, monthly, by HS)
trade by US state (origin of exports / state of destination of imports)
trade by port and customs district
year-to-date cumulative and general vs consumption imports
sources.accessMethod: REST API (HTTPS GET, JSON or CSV via &outputFormat=csv)
sources.auth: free token (Census API key, instant signup at api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html)
sources.cadence: monthly (FT-900 release, ~5 weeks after month end)
sources.whyItMatters: The single most important new trade source: authoritative US-government bilateral volume with both HS-product and US-state cuts, updated monthly, JSON/CSV, one free key. Directly answers 'how much does Mexico trade with the US, in what, from where.'
sources.terms: US federal public-domain data; free to use with attribution to US Census Bureau

sources.name: USITC DataWeb API (US trade + tariff treatment integrated)
sources.whatItGives: US imports/exports integrated WITH the tariff and customs treatment on each flow — customs value, landed duty-paid value, calculated duties, and dutiable value by HTS number, country, and Census program. Uniquely joins trade volume to what was actually paid in tariffs.
keyIndicators: US imports from Mexico by HTS8/HTS10
trade under specific programs (e.g., USMCA claims)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (HTTPS POST JSON query, Bearer token)
sources.endpoint: https://dataweb.usitc.gov/ (API tab for token); query API docs https://www.usitc.gov/sites/default/files/applications/dataweb/api/dataweb_query_api.html
sources.auth: free token (create DataWeb account, generate Bearer token in API tab)
sources.whyItMatters: The only official source that ties Mexico-US trade volume to duties actually collected — lets the Brief show effective realized tariff burden, not just statutory rates. Complements Census with the tariff dimension.
sources.terms: US federal public data; free with USITC attribution

sources.name: USITC Harmonized Tariff Schedule (HTS) reststop API
sources.whatItGives: The current legal US import tariff schedule as machine-readable data: for any HTS range, the Column 1 General (MFN) rate, Special (FTA/USMCA) rates, Column 2 rates, and the Chapter 99 provisions that carry Section 232 (steel/aluminum/copper), Section 301 (China), and IEEPA/reciprocal tariff overlays. This is the APPLIED and enacted-additional tariff RATE source.
keyIndicators: MFN (Column 1 General) duty rate by HTS
sources.accessMethod: REST API (HTTPS GET; JSON, CSV or XLSX) + full-schedule bulk download
sources.cadence: per HTS revision (multiple times/year; ad hoc when tariff actions take effect — e.g., 2026 Rev 10)
sources.whyItMatters: The authoritative, no-key, JSON source for the actual US tariff RATES applied to Mexican goods, including the Section 232/301/IEEPA surcharges that dominate 2025-26 news. Pairs statutory rate with the trade volume from Census/DataWeb.
sources.terms: US government public-domain data; free

sources.name: Federal Register API v1 (tariff proclamations & USTR/Commerce actions)
sources.whatItGives: Structured, dated, primary-source access to every presidential proclamation, executive order, and USTR/Commerce notice that CREATES, MODIFIES, THREATENS, or ENDS a tariff — Section 232, Section 301, IEEPA/reciprocal tariffs, exclusions. Full-text search + metadata, with a one-tap link to the official document. This is the THREATENED/announced-rate source (rates appear here before/at enactment).
sources.accessMethod: REST API (HTTPS GET, JSON; filter by agency=ustr / dept-of-commerce, term=tariff, publication date)
sources.whyItMatters: The Brief's honesty law needs the PRIMARY document behind every tariff headline. This is where threatened and enacted Mexico tariff actions are officially published, dated, and linkable — the citation layer for the whole tariff story. Link-out, no republishing needed.
sources.terms: US government public-domain; free, generous rate limits

sources.name: UN Comtrade API (bilateral mirror trade, both sides)
sources.whatItGives: Both-sided (mirror) bilateral merchandise trade: Mexico-reported and US-reported (and any partner) flows by HS commodity, annual and monthly. Lets the Brief cross-check US figures against Mexico's own reporting and place Mexico-US trade in a global context (China, Canada, EU shares).
keyIndicators: Mexico reported exports/imports to US by HS
Mexico's trade with all partners (China, Canada, EU)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (HTTPS GET, JSON)
sources.endpoint: Free preview: https://comtradeapi.un.org/public/v1/preview/C/A/HS (typeCode/freqCode/classification); full data + keys via https://comtradedeveloper.un.org/ ; M49 codes: Mexico=484, USA=842
sources.auth: free token for full access (free preview endpoint needs no key but caps 500 records/call)
sources.cadence: monthly and annual (as reporters submit; typically 1-2 month lag for monthly)
sources.whyItMatters: Independent international cross-check on US Census numbers and the way to show Mexico's trade beyond the US (e.g., China exposure). Mirror data surfaces discrepancies worth flagging. The free-key preview is enough to prototype the connector.
sources.cost: free (paid premium tiers exist for high-volume/bulk)
sources.terms: UN open data; free with attribution; free tier has rate/record limits

sources.name: INEGI BCMM / Banco de Indicadores API (Mexico-authority trade balance)
sources.whatItGives: Mexico's OWN official merchandise trade statistics (Balanza Comercial de Mercancías de México) from the national statistics agency: total exports/imports, trade balance, oil vs non-oil, manufacturing (incl. automotive) exports, and the monthly headline print Mexican media cite.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (Banco de Indicadores / BIE, HTTPS GET, JSON or XML by indicator ID)
sources.auth: free token (register for INEGI API token)
sources.cadence: monthly (BCMM release ~27 days after month end)
sources.whyItMatters: The Mexican primary source — non-US-outsider readers trust INEGI's own numbers, and it gives the headline trade-balance figure and automotive/manufacturing export detail that US Census does not frame the same way. Balances the US-centric sources.
sources.terms: INEGI open data; free with attribution

sources.name: Mexico SIAVI / SNICE (Mexican import tariff schedule by fracción)
sources.whatItGives: Mexico's side of the tariff wall: for any Mexican tariff fraction (TIGIE 8-digit) and country of origin, the general (MFN) duty rate, treaty-preferential rates (incl. T-MEC/USMCA), non-tariff regulations, prior permits, and applicable NOMs — plus annual import/export statistics per fraction. The source for what Mexico charges on US (and Chinese) goods.
keyIndicators: Mexican general/MFN import duty by fracción
sources.accessMethod: Stable scrape (SIAVI 5.0 web pages by fracción; no public API)
sources.cadence: updated on TIGIE/DOF tariff-law changes (ad hoc); statistics annual
sources.whyItMatters: Symmetry: the tariff story is two-sided. When Mexico raises tariffs on Chinese goods or applies retaliatory duties on US products, this is the primary Mexican-government rate source. Fills the Mexican-tariff gap that US HTS/WITS cannot.
sources.terms: Secretaria de Economia public data; free (scrape responsibly, cache)

sources.name: World Bank WITS / UNCTAD TRAINS API (applied + preferential tariff rates)
sources.whatItGives: Harmonized applied-MFN, bound, and preferential tariff rates for 160+ economies including Mexico, at HS6, from UNCTAD TRAINS + WTO IDB — plus tariff profile indicators (simple/weighted average tariff, % duty-free). Best for comparable cross-country tariff levels and Mexico's applied rate schedule in one normalized dataset.
keyIndicators: Mexico applied MFN tariff (simple & trade-weighted avg)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (SDMX and URL-based; XML or JSON)
sources.whyItMatters: Distinct from the already-wired World Bank macro API — this is the tariff product. Gives normalized, comparable tariff averages so the Brief can say 'Mexico's average applied tariff is X vs the US's Y,' and track structural (not headline) tariff levels.
sources.terms: World Bank/UNCTAD open data; free with attribution

sources.name: WTO Timeseries API (trade + market-access/tariff indicators)
sources.whatItGives: WTO flagship statistical API: merchandise and services trade (annual/quarterly/monthly), plus market-access indicators — bound, MFN-applied, and preferential tariffs — and non-tariff information for 150+ economies. Authoritative multilateral framing and services-trade coverage that goods-only sources lack.
keyIndicators: bound vs applied vs preferential tariff averages
sources.accessMethod: REST API (HTTPS GET, JSON; account-based key)
sources.endpoint: https://apiportal.wto.org/ (Timeseries API); portal https://timeseries.wto.org/ ; new tariff platform https://ttd.wto.org/en/analysis/tariff-timeseries
sources.auth: free token (register on WTO API portal for subscription key)
sources.whyItMatters: Adds the multilateral, WTO-official layer and services trade (Mexico's services balance, tourism) that Census/Comtrade goods data miss. Its tariff timeseries is a second independent tariff-rate cross-check against WITS.
sources.terms: WTO open data; free with attribution and key

sources.name: BEA International Trade API (services trade + country facts)
sources.whatItGives: US Bureau of Economic Analysis data on US international trade in goods AND services by country (IntlServTrade dataset, 90+ countries incl. Mexico) and Country Fact Sheets — the services-trade dimension (travel, transport, IP, financial) and FDI that customs-based sources omit.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (HTTPS GET, JSON or XML; dataset IntlServTrade / ITA)
sources.endpoint: https://apps.bea.gov/api/signup/ (register + docs); fact sheets https://apps.bea.gov/international/factsheet/ ; user guide https://apps.bea.gov/api/_pdf/bea_web_service_api_user_guide.pdf
sources.auth: free token (BEA API key, instant signup)
sources.whyItMatters: Completes the trade picture beyond merchandise: US-Mexico services and investment flows, from the authoritative US economic-accounts agency. Easy JSON + free key. Complements Census (goods) with services/FDI.
sources.terms: US federal public-domain; free with BEA attribution

sources.name: CBP Cargo Systems Messaging Service (CSMS) — operational tariff guidance feed
sources.whatItGives: US Customs' operational bulletins telling importers exactly how tariffs are being COLLECTED right now: implementation of new Section 232/301/IEEPA/reciprocal duties, HTS Chapter 99 filing instructions, effective dates, stacking rules, and exclusion mechanics. The real-time 'what CBP is actually charging today' layer between a proclamation and the schedule.
keyIndicators: effective dates & stacking rules for Mexico-relevant duties
sources.accessMethod: RSS/bulletin feed (GovDelivery) + web archive scrape
sources.cadence: as published (multiple messages/week; hourly-fresh during active tariff changes)
sources.whyItMatters: The freshest official signal on tariffs in force — CBP publishes collection instructions the day duties take effect, ahead of schedule revisions. Turns a legal proclamation into 'the rate on this Mexican product changed today.' High news value, fully citable, link-out only.
sources.terms: US government public messages; free (link-out, no republishing)

sources.name: Data Mexico trade cubes — deeper HS x state pull (ALREADY WIRED)
sources.whatItGives: Secretaria de Economia's Data Mexico OLAP already exposes Mexico trade by state and product; going deeper into the trade cubes yields state-level export/import by HS and subnational partner detail without a new connector.
keyIndicators: economic complexity of state export baskets
sources.accessMethod: OLAP API (already integrated)
sources.endpoint: https://api.datamexico.org/ (Tesseract OLAP cubes; already in the stack)
sources.whyItMatters: Not a new source — flagged as a duplicate of the already-wired Data Mexico connector. Noting it so the team pulls the deeper trade cubes (state x HS x partner) from the existing integration rather than adding redundant plumbing.
sources.terms: Data Mexico open data; free with attribution

domain: US-side data that moves Mexico (US indicators driving the peso, Mexican exports, and cross-border trade)
notes: Verified reachable/real via WebSearch + WebFetch on 2026-07-09. gov docs pages (fred, bls) return 403 to automated fetchers but the APIs themselves are live, well-documented, and widely used — confirmed via search + official doc URLs. Priority: all are official/primary and free except ISM (proprietary) and ICE DXY (proprietary) which are flagged. Two proprietary items are included deliberately because Alan's directive named them (DXY, ISM/CFTC) — each ships with a free official substitute. Biggest wins for breadth-beyond-what's-wired: FRED (one connector unlocks ~800k US series incl. UST yields, Fed funds, INDPRO, US auto sales, US CPI, broad dollar index), Federal Register API (live US tariff/executive-order feed — directly answers the tariffs ask, no auth), and CFTC COT (peso speculative positioning). Duplicate flags: US Census Intl Trade + BEA ITA mirror Data Mexico's trade from the US side (complementary — higher frequency, US-reported, and captures US-demand shocks Data Mexico lags); FRED broad dollar index overlaps Banxico's FX direction but is complementary (US-side driver vs. the peso outcome). Recommended connector count: FRED alone covers ~8 of Alan's named series (DGS10, DGS2, FEDFUNDS, INDPRO, RSAFS, CPIAUCSL, TOTALSA/US auto sales, DTWEXBGS) — build that first, it's the highest leverage single connector.

sources.name: FRED API (Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis)
sources.whatItGives: The master US macro feed — one free connector unlocks ~800k+ time series aggregated from 100+ sources, including nearly every US indicator Alan named. Ideal single highest-leverage build.
keyIndicators: US total light-vehicle sales (TOTALSA) — drives Mexican auto exports
Nominal Broad US Dollar Index (DTWEXBGS) — free DXY substitute
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/XML/CSV via file_type param)
sources.cadence: series-dependent: daily (yields, FX), monthly (CPI, IP, retail, auto sales); API updated as source releases post
sources.whyItMatters: US Treasury yields and Fed funds set the peso carry trade; US IP/retail/auto sales are the demand pull for Mexican manufacturing exports; the broad dollar index is the single cleanest driver of USDMXN. One connector, dozens of the most peso-relevant series.
sources.terms: Free for any use; FRED terms require attribution to source + St. Louis Fed; data is US-government public domain underneath.

sources.name: EIA Open Data API v2 (US Energy Information Administration)
sources.whatItGives: US energy prices and volumes — crude, refined products, natural gas — including Mexican crude (Maya) import flows into the US.
keyIndicators: US imports of Mexican crude (by origin facet)
sources.accessMethod: REST API v2 (hierarchical routes, JSON), key as query param
sources.whyItMatters: Pemex/Mexican fiscal revenue and the peso are oil-sensitive; Henry Hub gas is Mexico's imported power feedstock; US crude imports of Maya track a core Mexican export. Verified live: base https://api.eia.gov/v2/, 5,000-row cap per call with pagination.
sources.terms: Free; US-gov public domain; attribution requested.

sources.name: BLS Public Data API v2 (US Bureau of Labor Statistics)
sources.whatItGives: Authoritative US inflation and labor releases at the primary source (FRED mirrors many of these but BLS gives you the raw release the instant it prints, with full series granularity).
sources.accessMethod: REST API v2 (POST JSON body of series IDs)
sources.auth: free token (registration raises limit to 500 queries/day, 50 series/query)
sources.cadence: monthly on fixed release calendar (CPI, jobs report)
sources.whyItMatters: US CPI and the monthly jobs report are the two biggest scheduled peso-volatility events (they set Fed expectations). Wiring the primary source lets the brief flag the release on print, not on a mirror's lag.
sources.terms: Free; US-gov public domain; attribution requested.

sources.name: CFTC Commitments of Traders — Public Reporting (Socrata SODA API)
sources.whatItGives: Weekly speculative positioning in CME Mexican Peso futures — long/short/net by trader category — the cleanest read on how leveraged funds are betting on the peso.
keyIndicators: Mexican Peso non-commercial (managed money) net position
sources.accessMethod: REST / Socrata SODA (JSON, SoQL filters $where/$select), no auth
sources.endpoint: https://publicreporting.cftc.gov/resource/jun7-fc8e.json (Legacy Combined; also TFF & Disaggregated datasets under publicreporting.cftc.gov)
sources.auth: none (optional free Socrata app token to raise throttle)
sources.cadence: weekly (released Fridays ~3:30pm ET, as-of prior Tuesday)
sources.whyItMatters: Peso net spec positioning is a leading sentiment/mean-reversion signal watched by every USDMXN desk; extreme positioning precedes sharp unwinds. Verified: open Socrata endpoint, no auth, 680k+ historical rows, filter to the Mexican Peso contract.
sources.terms: Free; US-gov public domain; open data policy.

sources.name: US Treasury Fiscal Data API
sources.whatItGives: Treasury's own open API — daily par yield curve, rates of exchange (incl. official Treasury USD/MXN reference), and fiscal/debt series — all with no authentication.
keyIndicators: Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates (all tenors)
Average interest rates on Treasury debt
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/CSV/XML, rich fields/filter/pagination params)
sources.whyItMatters: The full US yield curve is the risk-free benchmark the peso carry trade is priced against; the Treasury's own reference exchange rate is a citable official USD/MXN. No-auth means a trivial connector. Verified open, JSON default.
sources.terms: Free; open data, no registration.

sources.name: BEA API (US Bureau of Economic Analysis)
sources.whatItGives: US GDP/PCE plus the International Transactions Accounts (ITA) — the US-side, authoritative record of US-Mexico trade in goods AND services and direct investment.
keyIndicators: US foreign direct investment position in Mexico (MNE/IIP)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (single URI, querystring params; JSON/XML)
sources.auth: free token (36-char UserID; no disposable emails)
sources.whyItMatters: US GDP/consumer demand is the ultimate pull for Mexican exports; ITA captures services + FDI that goods-only trade feeds miss; US FDI position in Mexico is a nearshoring gauge. Verified live, JSON.
sources.terms: Free; US-gov public domain; attribution requested.

sources.name: Federal Register API v1 (US National Archives)
sources.whatItGives: Live, structured feed of every US executive action — presidential proclamations, executive orders, and agency rules — the primary source for US tariff and trade-policy actions hitting Mexico. Directly answers the tariffs ask.
keyIndicators: publication date + full-text link per document
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON), full-text + faceted search (agency, doc type, presidential_document_type, date)
sources.whyItMatters: US tariff announcements are the single largest political driver of the peso in 2025-26; this is the official, dated, link-outable primary source (fails-closed and no republishing needed — POINT + link). Filter presidential_document_type=proclamation + keyword 'Mexico'/'tariff'. Verified no-auth JSON.
sources.terms: Free; US-gov public domain; no key.

sources.name: US Census Bureau International Trade API
sources.whatItGives: Monthly official US imports from / exports to Mexico by HS commodity, NAICS industry, US port, and state — the US-reported mirror of the bilateral trade relationship.
keyIndicators: US imports from Mexico by HS code (CTY_CODE=2010)
US exports to Mexico by HS/NAICS
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON array), get/time/CTY_CODE params
sources.whyItMatters: US-side trade data lands on a fixed schedule and captures US-demand shocks; port/state granularity supports nearshoring and border-throughput stories. Verified endpoints live.
sources.terms: Free; US-gov public domain; attribution requested.

sources.name: Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas — Economic Data & Mexico Reports
sources.whatItGives: The Fed district that specializes in Mexico and the border economy — Texas manufacturing/service-sector outlook surveys plus dedicated Mexico economic updates and the Texas-Mexico trade lens.
keyIndicators: Texas Manufacturing Outlook Survey (production, new orders)
Dallas Fed Mexico economic update indicators
sources.accessMethod: CSV/Excel data downloads + structured report pages (stable-URL scrape); some series re-exposed via FRED (Dallas Fed as source)
sources.endpoint: https://www.dallasfed.org/research/economics + surveys at https://www.dallasfed.org/research/surveys (many series also on FRED under Dallas Fed)
sources.whyItMatters: The Texas Manufacturing Outlook is an early read on cross-border supply-chain demand; Dallas Fed is the most Mexico-attentive US institution. Cleanest path: pull the Dallas Fed survey series via the FRED connector; scrape the report pages for the Mexico-specific narrative/figures.
sources.terms: Free; US-gov public domain; attribution requested.

sources.name: US Customs & Border Protection — Border Wait Times API
sources.whatItGives: Live commercial and passenger vehicle wait times at every US-Mexico land port of entry — a real-time proxy for cross-border trade throughput and friction.
keyIndicators: commercial truck lane wait times (Laredo, Otay Mesa, El Paso, etc.)
sources.accessMethod: Public XML/JSON feed (documented open feed)
sources.whyItMatters: Commercial wait times at Laredo (largest US-Mexico trade port) spike when trade is disrupted or surging — a high-frequency, unique signal no macro feed provides. Verify field schema on first pull; endpoint path may need confirming against the live portal.
sources.terms: Free; US-gov public domain.

sources.name: ISM Manufacturing PMI (Institute for Supply Management)
sources.whatItGives: US Manufacturing PMI — a leading indicator of US factory demand that leads Mexican manufacturing export orders by ~1-2 months.
sources.accessMethod: No free official API — headline via monthly press-release page (stable-URL scrape) on release day; historical requires ISM subscription
sources.auth: paid (full data); headline number free from release page
sources.whyItMatters: ISM New Orders leads Mexican export orders and is a top-tier peso event; but ISM stopped licensing to FRED and gates data behind a paywall. Practical wire: scrape the headline + New Orders from the release page monthly (POINT-style single figure), or substitute the free S&P Global US Manufacturing PMI / FRED regional Fed surveys as a proxy.
sources.terms: Proprietary — ISM copyright; only the reported headline figure may be cited with attribution (do NOT ingest full report). Fails the 'free official' bar; included because Alan named it — flag as paid/scrape.

sources.name: ICE US Dollar Index (DXY) — with free FRED substitute
sources.whatItGives: The dollar-strength benchmark Alan named. The true ICE DXY is proprietary/paywalled; the wireable, free, official equivalent is the Fed's Nominal Broad US Dollar Index via FRED.
keyIndicators: SUBSTITUTE: Nominal Broad US Dollar Index (DTWEXBGS, daily)
sources.accessMethod: DXY: proprietary (ICE/vendor, no free API). Substitute: REST via FRED (JSON)
sources.endpoint: Substitute: https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations?series_id=DTWEXBGS (ICE DXY marketed at https://www.ice.com/, no free feed)
sources.auth: DXY: paid vendor license. Substitute: free FRED token
sources.whyItMatters: Dollar strength is a primary USDMXN driver, but DXY itself is not freely/officially licensable — the Fed broad dollar index is a free, official, citable, daily substitute (and arguably better for Mexico since it includes the peso's trade weights). Wire DTWEXBGS via the FRED connector; skip the proprietary DXY.
sources.terms: DXY: proprietary, license required (do not ingest). Substitute (FRED/Fed): free, public domain, attribution.

domain: Political polling & governance (Mexico) — approval trackers, poll aggregators, electoral/congressional data, and democracy/rule-of-law/corruption indices

sources.name: Oraculus — Poll of Polls + Aprobación Presidencial (Sheinbaum approval aggregator)
sources.whatItGives: The de facto Mexican poll-of-polls. A Bayesian house-effect-adjusted aggregate of all public presidential-approval and vote-intention polls, plus a tracked Sheinbaum approval/disapproval time series updated as new polls drop. This is THE single most important governance signal for the Brief — one number that summarizes the whole polling field.
keyIndicators: poll-of-polls vote intention by party (during election cycles)
sources.accessMethod: scrape (stable HTML page; charts are JS-rendered so read the underlying data payload / rendered table)
sources.cadence: updated whenever a new major poll is published (roughly weekly/monthly); approval series is monthly-ish
sources.terms: Third-party private aggregator, no explicit open license. Treat as POINT/link-out per honesty law: cite the datapoint + date + link to Oraculus, do not republish their full dataset. Attribution to Oraculus required.
sources.whyItMatters: Approval is the master governance variable for an outsider reading Mexico — it moves markets, reform odds, and US-Mexico dynamics. Oraculus is the trusted synthesis that avoids cherry-picking any single house.

sources.name: El Financiero — Encuesta EF (monthly presidential approval tracker)
sources.whatItGives: A consistent, long-running monthly national approval poll with methodology and topical breakdowns (approval on security, corruption, economy, social programs; 'main problem facing Mexico'). One of the most-cited single-house trackers; very regular cadence makes it ideal for a monthly refresh.
keyIndicators: approval by policy area (security, economy, corruption, social programs)
sources.accessMethod: scrape monthly article (predictable URL pattern /nacional/YYYY/MM/DD/...aprobacion...); also has an approval tag feed
sources.cadence: monthly (first week of each month, reporting prior month)
sources.terms: Copyrighted news content — POINT/link-out only: extract the headline figure + date, cite and link the article, never republish body text. Consistent with the Brief's news-as-point rule.
sources.whyItMatters: Gives a single-house monthly series with policy-area breakdowns Oraculus doesn't surface (e.g. only 13% approve of the fight against organized crime) — the texture behind the headline approval number.

sources.name: Consulta Mitofsky — Evaluación de Gobierno (monthly, for El Economista)
sources.whatItGives: Mexico's most established pollster's monthly government-evaluation tracker, with approval broken out by region, gender, education, and social-program recipiency, plus 'main problem' and 'main achievement/failure' series. Long historical series (multiple administrations) enables cross-sexenio comparison.
keyIndicators: approval among social-program recipients vs non-recipients
perceived main problem / main government achievement & failure
sources.accessMethod: scrape (mitofsky.mx evaluation page + monthly posts); some numbers only in embedded images/PDF
sources.cost: free (headline results public; full datasets are paid/subscriber)
sources.terms: Private pollster. Public results are POINT/link-out — cite figure + date + link. Full microdata is commercial. Do not republish.
sources.whyItMatters: The deepest demographic cross-tabs of any free tracker (the social-program-recipient split is central to understanding Morena's coalition). Adds an independent house to triangulate against El Financiero and Oraculus.

sources.name: Buendía & Márquez — Encuestas Públicas (national approval & governance surveys)
sources.whatItGives: High-credibility independent pollster (publishes with El Universal). National in-home surveys on presidential approval, direction of the country, leader-attribute batteries (honest, capable, firm character, empathetic), and issue polling. Structured, methodologically transparent releases.
sources.accessMethod: scrape (buendiaymarquez.org posts; each survey has its own slug e.g. /2408-aprobacion/)
sources.cadence: periodic (roughly quarterly + event-driven; e.g. 100-days, milestone surveys)
sources.terms: Private pollster; results published openly with methodology. POINT/link-out, attribute to Buendía & Márquez.
sources.whyItMatters: Adds a third independent, respected house so the Brief isn't reliant on any single pollster — and the right-track/wrong-track + attribute batteries capture sentiment dimensions approval alone misses.

sources.name: INE — Datos Abiertos + SICEE (electoral results & citizen-participation statistics)
sources.whatItGives: Official election authority's open data: full presidential/senate/deputy/gubernatorial/municipal AND the new judicial-election results down to the individual polling-station (casilla) level, 2015–2024, plus historical series back to 1991 and turnout ('conteos censales de participación') 2009–2024. Downloadable CSV/XLSX per electoral process.
keyIndicators: vote share by party & candidate (all levels)
sources.accessMethod: CSV/XLSX download (SICEE per-process exports); Datos Abiertos catalog pages
sources.cadence: per electoral event (results finalized weeks after each election); turnout dataset updated per cycle
sources.terms: Official Mexican government open data — freely usable and citable, primary source. Ideal 'one tap from source' authority.
sources.whyItMatters: The bedrock of electoral governance — turns the polling narrative into hard outcomes, and uniquely includes the controversial 2025 judicial-election results (the reform outsiders most want to understand). Primary, dated, official.

sources.name: Cámara de Diputados (SITL) + Senado — Congress composition by party (bancadas)
sources.whatItGives: Official current composition of both chambers: seats per party (grupo parlamentario) in the 500-seat Chamber of Deputies and 128-seat Senate, member rosters, and legislature metadata. Determines whether the ruling coalition holds a simple vs qualified (2/3) majority — the single fact that governs constitutional-reform capacity.
keyIndicators: coalition total vs 2/3 supermajority threshold (334 deputies / 86 senators)
sources.accessMethod: scrape (structured roster pages; SITL member DB)
sources.cadence: per legislature (3-yr Chamber / 6-yr Senate) plus intra-term changes when members switch bancada
sources.terms: Official government source, freely citable. Primary and authoritative.
sources.whyItMatters: Approval means little without legislative power. Whether Morena+allies clear two-thirds is THE governance variable behind the 2024–25 constitutional-reform wave (judicial reform, autonomous-body elimination). A must-have derived indicator: 'supermajority: yes/no'.

sources.name: Sistema de Información Legislativa (SIL / SEGOB) — legislative tracking & numeralia
sources.whatItGives: SEGOB's official system tracking every initiative in the Congreso de la Unión: bills introduced, status, sponsor, executive-branch initiatives, and per-legislator productivity ('numeralia'). Lets the Brief show what reforms are moving through Congress in near-real time, not just outcomes.
keyIndicators: count/status of initiatives (introduced, in committee, approved)
sources.accessMethod: scrape (structured numeralia and asuntos-legislativos pages)
sources.cadence: continuous during legislative sessions; numeralia updated regularly
sources.terms: Official government (SEGOB) source, freely citable. Data sourced from the official gazettes.
sources.whyItMatters: Governance is a flow, not a snapshot. SIL surfaces the reform pipeline (what Sheinbaum's coalition is pushing right now) — the leading indicator of the policy changes that move markets and migration/energy/security policy.

sources.name: V-Dem — Varieties of Democracy dataset (democracy indices)
sources.whatItGives: The most granular academic democracy dataset: 500+ indicators / 250+ indices covering electoral, liberal, participatory, deliberative and egalitarian democracy, with long historical Mexico series. Captures democratic backsliding (judicial independence, checks on the executive, media freedom) that annual scores miss.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download (full dataset ZIP) + official R package (vdemdata); country-graph web tool
sources.terms: Free for research/non-commercial with citation; well-documented codebook and suggested citation. Cite V-Dem + version. Confirm terms for commercial redistribution.
sources.whyItMatters: The rigorous, multi-dimensional answer to 'is Mexican democracy eroding?' — outsiders' top governance question given the judicial-reform and autonomous-body changes. Downloadable and stable = trivially wireable annually.

sources.name: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index
sources.whatItGives: Cross-country rule-of-law scores (143+ countries) across 8 factors from household + expert surveys: constraints on government power, absence of corruption, order & security, fundamental rights, civil & criminal justice. Mexico-specific factor/sub-factor scores with global & regional rank.
keyIndicators: overall Rule of Law score & rank
8 factor scores (esp. absence of corruption, criminal justice, constraints on gov power)
sources.accessMethod: CSV/Excel + PDF download from WJP; ALSO available via World Bank Data360 unified API (dataset WJP_ROL)
sources.terms: WJP data available for research/journalism with attribution; publishes an open-data page. Cite World Justice Project + year. Data360 mirror simplifies wiring.
sources.whyItMatters: Rule of law is the governance dimension foreign investors and firms weigh most (contract enforcement, corruption, judicial predictability). WJP's criminal-justice and corruption factors directly frame Mexico's security-and-impunity story with a comparable, dated number.

sources.name: Transparency International — Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI)
sources.whatItGives: Annual 0–100 corruption-perception score and global rank for Mexico, with a full time-series (comparable ~2012–present). The single most-recognized corruption benchmark worldwide.
sources.accessMethod: XLSX/CSV download (official full-results file) + mirrors (datahub.io CSV, Our World in Data)
sources.terms: CPI licensed CC BY-ND 4.0 — free to use and republish WITH attribution but NO derivatives of the dataset (present scores as-is). Attribution to Transparency International required. Fits honesty law well.
sources.whyItMatters: Corruption is a top-tier governance and investment-risk variable for outsiders; CPI is the universally cited yardstick, dated and one-tap-to-source. The CC BY-ND license actually permits showing the number directly (unlike news).

sources.name: Freedom House — Freedom in the World (Mexico scores)
sources.whatItGives: Annual Political Rights (0–40) and Civil Liberties (0–60) scores, aggregate score, and Free/Partly Free status for Mexico, with a 1973–present country-ratings time series and granular sub-indicator (A1–G4) breakdown. Includes a narrative country report.
keyIndicators: sub-scores incl. electoral process, judicial independence, rule of law
sources.accessMethod: XLSX download (country-and-territory ratings 1973–latest; all-data 2013–latest)
sources.terms: Free for personal/academic/nonprofit use; COMMERCIAL use requires prior written permission (email FIW Data Request). Flag this for the Brief's licensing review if monetized.
sources.whyItMatters: A second, differently-constructed democracy benchmark to corroborate V-Dem — and its 2026 downgrade note (score cut over Morena interference in judicial elections) is exactly the backsliding signal outsiders track. Sub-indicators map cleanly to governance topics.

sources.name: INEGI — Indicadores API (governance & security perception: ENVIPE / ENSU / ENCIG)
sources.whatItGives: Official statistics-office REST API exposing the government/security-perception survey programs: ENVIPE (annual national victimization & security perception), ENSU (quarterly urban insecurity perception), and ENCIG (government quality/impact & corruption-experience). Objective official measures of how citizens rate government performance, safety, and corruption in services.
keyIndicators: corruption-experience rate in public procedures & satisfaction with public services (ENCIG)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/JSONP/XML), free token — INEGI Indicadores (BISE/BIE) v2.0
sources.terms: Official Mexican government open data — free, citable, primary. Token required but free. Excellent 'one tap from source' authority.
sources.whyItMatters: Bridges polling (what people feel about the president) and hard governance outcomes (how unsafe they actually feel, how much corruption they personally experienced). ENSU's quarterly cadence gives a fresh, official, non-partisan governance pulse — and the same INEGI API unlocks dozens of other official series.

sources.name: World Bank Data360 — unified governance API (WGI, CPI, WJP, and more)
sources.whatItGives: A single modern REST API that aggregates many governance datasets — Worldwide Governance Indicators, CPI, WJP Rule of Law, and others — under one query interface with structured metadata. Lets you pull multiple governance indices for Mexico from one endpoint instead of wiring each provider separately.
keyIndicators: WGI 6 dimensions (voice & accountability, political stability, gov effectiveness, regulatory quality, rule of law, control of corruption)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON) — data360_get_data(database_id, indicator_id, filters); search via data360_search_indicators
sources.endpoint: https://data360api.worldbank.org (base) · docs https://data360.worldbank.org/en/api · reference impl github.com/worldbank/data360-mcp
sources.cadence: follows each underlying source (WGI annual, CPI annual, WJP annual)
sources.terms: World Bank open data (CC BY 4.0 for WB-produced content; mirrored third-party datasets carry their own license — verify per indicator, e.g. CPI CC BY-ND). Primary-source links provided.
sources.whyItMatters: One connector, many governance indices — high leverage for 'more sources = differentiator.' The unified metadata (dated, source-linked) fits the honesty law and dramatically cuts wiring cost vs per-provider scrapers.

sources.name: World Bank — Worldwide Governance Indicators (WGI, direct REST)
sources.whatItGives: The canonical composite governance dataset: six dimensions scored ~-2.5 to +2.5 (and percentile rank) for Mexico, 1996–2024, drawing on 35 underlying sources. Available directly via the standard World Bank Indicators REST API.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/XML) — World Bank Indicators v2
sources.terms: World Bank open data, CC BY 4.0, freely citable.
sources.whyItMatters: Percentile-rank governance dimensions that let an outsider place Mexico against every peer economy in one glance — the standard institutional-quality read for investors. If the World Bank REST connector is already wired for GDP, adding these six indicator codes is near-zero marginal effort.

sources.name: Latinobarómetro — regional public-opinion survey (democracy & institutions)
sources.whatItGives: Annual 18-country Latin American opinion survey with a Mexico sample: support for democracy, satisfaction with democracy, interpersonal & institutional trust, and attitudes toward authoritarianism. Situates Mexican governance sentiment in a regional benchmark (high-vs-low comparison Alan wants).
keyIndicators: trust in Congress / government / political parties
'democracy preferable to any other form of government'
sources.accessMethod: microdata download (SPSS/Stata/SAS/R files by round) + online analysis tool
sources.terms: Free for research with registration and citation; redistribution of microdata restricted — extract Mexico-level aggregates and cite Latinobarómetro + year. Verify commercial-use terms.
sources.whyItMatters: Adds the regional-comparative dimension to governance sentiment (is Mexican support for democracy high or low vs the region?) — directly serves the benchmarked high-vs-low framing, from a respected long-running source.

notes:
Coverage: 15 sources spanning the full political-polling & governance domain — approval trackers (Oraculus aggregate + 3 independent houses: El Financiero, Mitofsky, Buendía & Márquez), hard electoral/legislative data (INE/SICEE results incl. 2025 judicial elections, Congress composition, SIL reform pipeline), and the four democracy/rule-of-law/corruption index families (V-Dem, WJP, TI-CPI, Freedom House) plus official INEGI governance-perception surveys and two World Bank governance APIs.

Wireability tiers: EASY (pure REST/download, wire first) — INEGI Indicadores API (free token), V-Dem, TI-CPI, Freedom House XLSX, World Bank WGI REST, World Bank Data360. MEDIUM (scrape structured HTML) — Oraculus, El Financiero, Mitofsky, Buendía & Márquez, INE/SICEE downloads, Congress composition, Latinobarómetro (registration). HARD — SIL (legislative pipeline, JS-heavy).

Duplicates flagged: (1) World Bank WGI direct REST and (2) Data360 both ride the already-wired World Bank source family — but expose governance indicator codes (RL.EST, CC.EST, etc.) and mirrored third-party indices (CPI, WJP) not currently pulled, so they extend rather than repeat. If the World Bank REST connector already exists, adding the six WGI codes is near-zero effort — recommend wiring those before standing up separate WJP/TI scrapers, and use Data360 as a single connector for CPI+WJP+WGI to cut cost.

Honesty-law fit: Official/primary sources (INE, INEGI, Congress, SIL, World Bank, V-Dem, Freedom House, WJP, TI) are all dated + one-tap-to-source and safe to display as figures. TI-CPI is CC BY-ND (show as-is, attribute). Freedom House is free for non-commercial only — FLAG for licensing review if the Brief is ever monetized. The four polling houses (Oraculus, El Financiero, Mitofsky, Buendía) are private/news — treat as POINT/link-out: surface the dated datapoint + link, never republish, always attribute the house.

Priority recommendation to wire first: (1) Oraculus approval aggregate — highest-signal single governance number; (2) INEGI Indicadores API — one free-token REST connector unlocks ENSU/ENVIPE/ENCIG + many more official series; (3) World Bank WGI codes via existing connector; (4) INE/SICEE electoral results incl. judicial elections; (5) Congress composition supermajority flag (334 deputies / 86 senators) — the derived indicator that explains the whole constitutional-reform wave. Not yet verified live: exact Data360 base host (data360api.worldbank.org) and Oraculus's underlying JSON payload structure — both should be confirmed at implementation.

Hunted the security/rule-of-law domain beyond SESNSP homicides. Prioritized official/primary + free. Key architecture note: Mexico has TWO parallel homicide counts — SESNSP (crime investigations, monthly, timely) and INEGI EDR (registered deaths by ICD-10, annual, definitive and ~higher); wiring both and showing the gap is itself a signal. Verified every endpoint via WebFetch/WebSearch. INEGI Banco de Indicadores API (REST, free token, JSON/XML) is the single highest-leverage connector — it exposes ENSU/ENVIPE/EDR indicator series programmatically, so several survey sources below can be pulled through one auth. SESNSP moved primary hosting to secretariadoejecutivo.gob.mx with SharePoint/OneDrive CSV+Excel links (link paths rotate monthly — connector should scrape the landing page for the current month's href rather than hardcode). Civil-society sources (ONC, Alto al Secuestro, Data Cívica) matter precisely because official extortion/kidnapping counts have ~93-99% underreporting; they are the counter-number. Duplicate flags: SESNSP homicides already wired — the incidencia dataset below is the SAME source but the FULL crime-type/municipio breakdown (extortion, femicide, kidnapping, robo a transportista, etc.), a major expansion not a dupe. ACLED/UCDP are conflict-EVENT data (actors, fatalities, geolocated), NOT a duplicate of GDELT news. Could not fully hunt: state fiscalía portals (FGJ-CDMX carpeta-level open data is excellent but per-state), TransBorder/US-side seizure data (CBP) — flag for a future US-moves-Mexico pass.

sources.name: SESNSP — Incidencia Delictiva del Fuero Común (full crime-type & municipio detail)
sources.whatItGives: The complete monthly crime dataset behind the homicide number already wired: opened investigations (carpetas) and victims broken out by ~40 crime subtypes, at national/state AND municipio level, one row per state-municipio-crime-month with 12 monthly columns per year. This is where extortion, feminicidio, secuestro, robo a transportista/robo de vehículo, and violent-vs-non-violent robbery live.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download (also .xlsx). Static file per methodological period; connector scrapes the landing page for the current month's SharePoint/OneDrive href, downloads the ZIP/CSV, parses wide→long.
sources.cadence: monthly (typically published ~20th for prior month; municipio file 2015→present, national series 1997→present)
sources.terms: Official government open data (Términos de Libre Uso de la Información — MX gob.mx). Cite SESNSP + snapshot date. Fully usable, attribute.
sources.whyItMatters: Turns the single homicide figure already wired into the entire crime surface — lets the Brief show extortion and cargo theft (the two crimes foreign investors and logistics actually price in) by state and city, one tap from the primary federal source.

sources.name: INEGI — Estadísticas de Defunciones Registradas (EDR): defunciones por homicidio
sources.whatItGives: The 'true' homicide count from death certificates coded to ICD-10 (X85–Y09) — independent of prosecutors' crime reports. Definitive annual series 1990→present by state of registration/occurrence, sex, age, means. 2024 preliminary: 33,241 presumed homicides, 25.6/100k.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (Banco de Indicadores, JSON/XML) for the headline indicator; plus OLAP cubes, interactive tabulados, and microdata CSV download for full disaggregation.
sources.endpoint: https://www.inegi.org.mx/programas/edr/ (OLAP: https://www.inegi.org.mx/sistemas/olap/Proyectos/bd/continuas/mortalidad/MortalidadGeneral.asp ; API via Banco de Indicadores below)
sources.auth: none for downloads; free token for the API
sources.cadence: annual (preliminary ~July/Aug, definitive later); series back to 1990
sources.terms: INEGI open data, free use with attribution. Cite INEGI EDR + year.
sources.whyItMatters: The credibility anchor: showing INEGI's death-certificate homicides next to SESNSP's investigation count exposes the undercount and is a signature 'we source everything' move no news outlet reproduces.

sources.name: INEGI — ENSU (Encuesta Nacional de Seguridad Pública Urbana)
sources.whatItGives: Quarterly perception-of-insecurity survey across ~90 cities: % of adults who feel unsafe in their city, and in specific settings (ATM, public transit, bank, streets), plus expectations. The fastest-cadence official read on how safe Mexicans FEEL — a leading indicator distinct from crime counts.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (Banco de Indicadores, JSON/XML) for the headline series; full microdata + tabulados as CSV download each quarter.
sources.auth: none for download; free token for API
sources.terms: INEGI open data, attribution. Cite ENSU + quarter.
sources.whyItMatters: Perception moves consumption, tourism and investment independent of actual crime; quarterly cadence makes it a live gauge the Brief can trend when annual surveys are stale.

sources.name: INEGI — ENVIPE (Encuesta Nacional de Victimización y Percepción sobre Seguridad Pública)
sources.whatItGives: The flagship annual victimization survey — measures the cifra negra (dark figure): total crimes actually suffered by households vs. those reported/investigated (~93% go unreported). National + state estimates of victimization prevalence, cost of crime (% GDP), reasons for not reporting, trust in police/prosecutors.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (Banco de Indicadores) for headline indicators; boletín PDF + full microdata CSV each year.
sources.auth: none for download; free token for API
sources.cadence: annual (September release, reference to prior year)
sources.terms: INEGI open data, attribution.
sources.whyItMatters: The single most-cited proof that official crime stats undercount reality; the cifra negra and cost-of-crime %GDP are exactly the kind of dated, sourced figures the Brief exists to surface.

sources.name: INEGI — ENVE (Encuesta Nacional de Victimización de Empresas)
sources.whatItGives: Business-victimization survey: crimes suffered by firms, prevalence/incidence by sector and firm size, cost to business, extortion of businesses, and — uniquely — insecurity in transporting goods on highways (the cargo-theft proxy). National + state.
keyIndicators: robo/insecurity en transporte de mercancía en carretera
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON microdata download + boletín; headline indicators via Banco de Indicadores API.
sources.terms: INEGI open data, attribution.
sources.whyItMatters: The only official read on how crime taxes business and cargo — directly answers the nearshoring/logistics investor's core question about operating risk in Mexico.

sources.name: INEGI — Banco de Indicadores API (security indicator gateway)
sources.whatItGives: One REST API that returns any INEGI indicator time series (JSON/XML) by indicator ID — the programmatic pipe for ENSU, ENVIPE, EDR homicides, and other security series, so several sources above collapse into one auth + one parser.
keyIndicators: any INEGI security series by ID (homicide rate, ENSU perception, ENVIPE victimization, etc.)
sources.accessMethod: REST API. URL pattern: https://www.inegi.org.mx/app/api/indicadores/desarrolladores/jsonxml/INDICATOR/{IdIndicador}/{lang}/{geo}/{recentOnly}/{source e.g. BISE}/2.0/{TOKEN}?type=json . Indicator IDs found via INEGI 'Constructor de Consultas'.
sources.cadence: matches underlying series (quarterly/annual); pull on schedule
sources.terms: INEGI API terms; free use with attribution. Token is per-application.
sources.whyItMatters: Highest-leverage connector in the domain — build it once and every INEGI security series (and dozens of macro/labor series elsewhere) becomes a scheduled JSON pull instead of a bespoke scrape.

sources.name: ACLED — Armed Conflict Location & Event Data (Mexico)
sources.whatItGives: Individually geolocated, dated political-violence events with actors, event/sub-event type, and fatalities — cartel clashes, attacks on officials/journalists, armed-group territorial violence. Mexico is a core-coverage country. Country/subnational profiles and trend series.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/CSV) with myACLED access key; also Data Export Tool for filtered CSV/JSON. Query by country=Mexico, date range, event_type.
sources.cost: free for non-commercial/research; commercial use needs a license — confirm terms for a public product
sources.terms: Free access under registration; attribution required (cite ACLED + access date). Commercial/redistribution terms exist — verify before republishing derived data. Point/link-out is safest.
sources.whyItMatters: The only near-real-time, actor-attributed, geolocated conflict feed — turns diffuse 'cartel violence' into mappable, dated events that pair with SESNSP counts for a security picture no official source gives.

sources.name: UCDP — Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), Mexico
sources.whatItGives: Academic gold-standard organized-violence events (state-based, non-state/cartel, one-sided), geolocated to village/day with low/best/high fatality estimates. Mexico included in the global set; strong for non-state (cartel-vs-cartel) conflict.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON, paginated) and bulk dataset download (CSV/Excel/R). Filter country=Mexico (Gleditsch-Ward id) / coordinates.
sources.cadence: annual full release + monthly 'candidate' (UCDP GED Candidate) updates
sources.terms: Free, open, academic. Attribution to UCDP + dataset version. Fully usable.
sources.whyItMatters: No-auth, citable academic counterpart to ACLED — good cross-check and, unlike ACLED, unambiguously free/open for a public product, so it can be the redistributable conflict layer.

sources.name: RNPDNO — Registro Nacional de Personas Desaparecidas y No Localizadas (CNB/SEGOB)
sources.whatItGives: Official register of disappeared/not-located persons — the defining rule-of-law crisis metric. Statistical dashboard by state, sex, age, year; cumulative and by status (still missing vs located). ~120k+ cumulative disappeared.
sources.accessMethod: Dashboard (versión estadística) — scrape/aggregated figures; cleaned open CSV via Data Cívica 'Volver a Desaparecer' (11 public variables). Official machine-readable API is limited, so Data Cívica is the practical wire.
sources.cadence: dashboard updated continuously; Data Cívica CSV refreshed periodically (roughly quarterly)
sources.terms: Official public data; Data Cívica CSV is CC-licensed, attribution required. Cite CNB/SEGOB (+ Data Cívica for the CSV).
sources.whyItMatters: Disappearances are the rule-of-law wound official crime counts miss entirely; a dated, sourced figure here is high-signal and rarely presented cleanly to outsiders.

sources.name: Observatorio Nacional Ciudadano (ONC) — Delitos de Alto Impacto
sources.whatItGives: Civil-society monthly analysis of high-impact crimes (homicide, femicide, kidnapping, extortion, robbery variants) built ON TOP of SESNSP data with rates, rankings and trend interpretation — plus an interactive observatory API/backend for state/municipio crime series.
sources.accessMethod: Interactive observatory (JSON backend, scrapeable) at delitosmexico.onc.org.mx; monthly PDF/Excel reports for narrative. Observatory returns series by crime/state/period.
sources.terms: Civil-society publication; cite ONC. Reports are analysis (point/link-out, don't republish full text). The observatory sits on public SESNSP data.
sources.whyItMatters: Independent, credible interpretation layer and a ready-made extortion/kidnapping observatory — useful as a second opinion on official numbers and a QA cross-check for our own SESNSP pipeline.

sources.name: Alto al Secuestro — kidnapping tracker
sources.whatItGives: NGO monthly kidnapping counts (cases + victims) by state and by administration, explicitly reconciled against official SESNSP figures to expose the undercount (e.g., claims official stats omit ~93% of victims). The go-to independent kidnapping number cited across Mexican media.
sources.accessMethod: Stable scrape of the Estadística page (charts/tables) + monthly press figures.
sources.terms: NGO data, public. Cite Alto al Secuestro. Figures/statistics are facts and citable; scrape the numeric tables, link out for context.
sources.whyItMatters: Kidnapping is among the most underreported crimes; the NGO-vs-official gap is exactly the 'two numbers, both sourced' contrast the Brief is built to show. Scrape is brittle (site is chart-heavy) — hence hard.

sources.name: Justice in Mexico — Data Center / OCVED (Organized Criminal Violence Event Data)
sources.whatItGives: University of San Diego project: curated datasets on organized-crime homicides (compiling Reforma Ejecutómetro, Milenio, SESNSP, INEGI) plus OCVED, an event-level dataset of organized-criminal violence incidents with documentation. Strong English-language analytical framing of cartel violence.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/spreadsheet download from the Data Center; OCVED via request form (emailed dataset + docs).
sources.auth: none (OCVED requires a request-form email step)
sources.cadence: annual / periodic (aligned to their reports); OCVED updated on release cycles
sources.terms: Academic, free with attribution (cite Justice in Mexico / OCVED). Request-gated OCVED and compiled third-party (Reforma/Milenio) sources mean link-out + cite rather than auto-refresh.
sources.whyItMatters: Best English-language, methodologically transparent bridge between multiple homicide sources and cartel-attributed violence — high value for an outsider audience, but request-gated/periodic so it's a reference layer, not a live feed.

notes: All primary/official except where noted. Big picture on Mexican energy data plumbing: (1) The 2024-2025 sector overhaul folded CRE + CNH into the new Comisión Nacional de Energía (CNE) — legacy CRE/CNH URLs still resolve and still serve the data, but expect gradual migration to cne.gob.mx and *.energia.gob.mx mirrors; wire the working endpoint and keep the CNE mirror as fallback. (2) datos.gob.mx migrated to a new "Ajolote" front-end but the old CKAN API (api/3/action/datastore_search) still answers with JSON and is the single best programmatic doorway to CFE/PEMEX/CNH/CRE datasets — treat CKAN as connective tissue, not a rival to the primary portals. (3) Most Mexican energy agencies do NOT offer a clean REST API — CENACE, PEMEX EBDI, SENER SIE, CNH SIH expose ASP.NET report pages with CSV/XLS/PDF download links and querystring params; these are stable, schedulable scrapes/CSV pulls, not JSON APIs. The two genuinely clean JSON/XML feeds here are the CRE fuel-price Azure feed and the EIA API. None of these duplicate what's already wired (Banxico is macro/FX, Data Mexico is trade/employment OLAP, World Bank is structural) — energy is a genuine gap. Only overlap flag: the datos.gob.mx CKAN connector is a generic portal that could technically re-serve some Data Mexico-adjacent tables, but for energy it reaches datasets Data Mexico does not carry. Recommended wire priority: CRE fuel feed (easy, high-signal, daily), EIA API (easy, nearshoring narrative), CENACE demand+PML (medium, the grid-dealbreaker series), CNH production (medium), then the rest.

sources.name: CRE / CNE Fuel Price Feed (gasolina & diésel, Azure XML)
sources.whatItGives: Station-level commercial retail prices for regular gasoline, premium gasoline and diesel reported by every permit-holder in Mexico, plus a companion registry of all service stations (name, brand, CRE id, lat/lng, address). Two linked XML files joined on place_id.
sources.accessMethod: REST (static XML endpoint)
sources.cadence: Regenerated automatically ~every 4 hours; official prices refresh daily ~18:00 CT
sources.terms: Official CRE open data (datos abiertos); free reuse with attribution to CRE/CNE. Third-party mirror petrointelligence.com/api.php offers the same data as free JSON/CSV if you want pre-parsed output.
sources.whyItMatters: Fuel prices are the single most politically and consumer-sensitive number in Mexico (gasolinazo memory) and feed inflation. Verified live and reachable this session — cleanest wireable energy feed in the country.

sources.name: EIA International Energy Data API v2 (Mexico) + U.S. Henry Hub
sources.whatItGives: U.S. Energy Information Administration's harmonized international series for Mexico: electricity generation/capacity, petroleum production/consumption/imports-exports, natural gas, coal, renewables, CO2 — plus U.S.-side series that move Mexico (Henry Hub natural gas spot, U.S. crude, cross-border gas exports to Mexico).
keyIndicators: Mexico electricity net generation & installed capacity
Henry Hub natural gas spot price
U.S. natural gas pipeline exports to Mexico
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON), v2
sources.endpoint: https://api.eia.gov/v2/  (register at https://www.eia.gov/opendata/ ; browse routes at https://www.eia.gov/opendata/browser/ ; Mexico country hub https://www.eia.gov/international/analysis/country/MEX)
sources.auth: free token (API key, appended as ?api_key= in URL)
sources.cadence: Monthly for most international series; daily/weekly for U.S. spot prices (Henry Hub)
sources.terms: U.S. public-domain government data; free, TOS requires key registration for usage monitoring. Attribution: U.S. EIA.
sources.whyItMatters: Gives an independent, standardized cross-check on Mexico's own (sometimes lagging) energy stats, and Henry Hub is THE input price for ~70% of Mexico's power gas — the nearshoring-grid story runs through it.

sources.name: CENACE — Precios Marginales Locales (PML) MDA & MTR
sources.whatItGives: Locational marginal electricity prices for the Wholesale Electricity Market (MEM) at every pricing node, split into energy/congestion/losses components, for the Day-Ahead Market (MDA) and Real-Time Market (MTR), across the three systems (SIN, Baja California, Baja California Sur). The core wholesale power price signal.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/PDF/HTML download via SW-PML report service (querystring params); public SIM area
sources.endpoint: https://www.cenace.gob.mx/SIM/VISTA/REPORTES/PreEnergiaSisMEM.aspx  (also PreEnerServConMDA.aspx / PreEnerServConMTR.aspx ; SW-PML manual: https://www.cenace.gob.mx/DocsMEM/2022-06-24%20Manual%20T%C3%A9cnico%20SW-PML.pdf ; dataset mirror datos.gob.mx/organization/cenace)
sources.cadence: Daily (published on completion of MDA and MTR market runs)
sources.terms: Public information from CENACE (state grid operator); open reuse. No formal JSON API — schedulable CSV pull with date params; SW-PML technical manual documents the file schema.
sources.whyItMatters: Wholesale power price + the congestion component are the clearest quantitative read on whether the grid can actually support nearshoring capacity — the dealbreaker Alan named.

sources.name: CENACE — Grid Demand & Real Demand Estimation
sources.whatItGives: Near-real-time and historical national grid electricity demand vs. forecast vs. available generation resources, for the Sistema Interconectado Nacional and full SEN. The live pulse of the power system, including reserve-margin tightness.
sources.accessMethod: Web report / dashboard with underlying data endpoints (scrape/XHR JSON behind graficademanda)
sources.cadence: Intraday (demand graphic near-real-time); Real Demand Estimation daily
sources.terms: Public CENACE operational data; open reuse. Data pulled from the chart's backing JSON/XHR or the report CSV export.
sources.whyItMatters: Reserve-margin tightness and demand-vs-capacity gaps are the early-warning signal for grid stress and blackout risk — a direct infrastructure-capacity indicator investors watch.

sources.name: CENACE — Energía Generada por Tipo de Tecnología (generation mix)
sources.whatItGives: Historical and forecast electricity generation broken down by technology type (gas/CCGT, coal, hydro, wind, solar, nuclear, fuel oil, etc.) for the national system — the fuel/clean-energy mix of the grid.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/HTML report download (SIM public area)
sources.terms: Public CENACE data; open reuse. Report-page CSV export, schedulable.
sources.whyItMatters: The generation mix is the factual counter to political narratives about renewables vs. CFE fossil dispatch, and shows how exposed power costs are to imported gas.

sources.name: CNH / CNIH — Sistema de Información de Hidrocarburos (well-level oil & gas production)
sources.whatItGives: Monthly production per well of crude oil, condensate and natural gas from 1930 to present, plus reserves, contracts/assignments, and petroleum activity — for all of Mexico. The authoritative upstream production database (now under CNE).
sources.accessMethod: CSV/database download (full DB export) + interactive dashboards
sources.endpoint: https://produccion.hidrocarburos.gob.mx/  ,  https://sih.hidrocarburos.gob.mx/  , Portal de Información Técnica https://portal.cnih.cnh.gob.mx/  , open-data mirror https://datos.gob.mx/busca/organization/cnh
sources.auth: none (some bulk downloads may require a free CNIH account)
sources.terms: Official CNH/CNIH open data; free reuse with attribution. Full-database CSV download available — schedulable.
sources.whyItMatters: Independent, granular check on PEMEX's own numbers; national crude+gas output is a fiscal and trade fundamental (PEMEX revenue → federal budget).

sources.name: PEMEX — Base de Datos Institucional (EBDI) + Indicadores Petroleros
sources.whatItGives: PEMEX's own operational statistics: crude production by region/type, refinery throughput and output, crude & product exports by destination, imports, natural gas — as interactive query cubes plus a monthly 'Indicadores Petroleros' bulletin (PDF + tables).
sources.accessMethod: Interactive DB cubes (querystring cvecua= params, CSV/XLS export) + monthly PDF bulletin
sources.endpoint: https://ebdi.pemex.com/bdi/bdiController.do?action=about  (e.g. crude by region ...?action=cuadro&cvecua=ECRURA ; refinery ...cvecua=RPROREF) ; monthly bulletin https://www.pemex.com/ri/publicaciones/indicadores%20petroleros/indicador.pdf ; open-data mirror datos.gob.mx/organization/pemex
sources.terms: PEMEX public/open data; free reuse with attribution. EBDI cubes export CSV/XLS by cube code (cvecua) — schedulable; the fixed-URL monthly PDF is also parseable.
sources.whyItMatters: Refinery throughput and product imports show Mexico's fuel self-sufficiency gap (Dos Bocas/Olmeca narrative); crude exports feed the trade balance already tracked via other feeds.

sources.name: SENER — Sistema de Información Energética (SIE)
sources.whatItGives: SENER's integrated national energy statistics warehouse: energy balances, production/consumption by fuel, electricity, hydrocarbons, prices — aggregated across sector entities. The official top-level energy-statistics portal with dashboards, tables and download.
sources.accessMethod: Web dashboards + data export (dissemination module); some series mirrored to datos.gob.mx
sources.terms: Official SENER validated statistics; public transparency data, free reuse with attribution. Export/download from dissemination module; no clean REST API — schedulable scrape or CSV pull.
sources.whyItMatters: The single official roll-up of the whole energy sector — useful as the authoritative cross-check layer above the individual agency feeds.

sources.name: CNE (ex-CRE) — Índice de Referencia Nacional de Precios de Gas Natural al Mayoreo (IPGN)
sources.whatItGives: National and regional (6-region) reference index of wholesale natural-gas transaction prices, plus commercialized volumes (GJ). The official domestic gas price benchmark.
sources.accessMethod: Web dashboard + CSV via datos.gob.mx dataset
sources.terms: Official CNE/CRE open data; free reuse with attribution. datos.gob.mx CKAN resource gives direct CSV — easy schedule.
sources.whyItMatters: Domestic gas price and the regional spread show where in Mexico gas (hence power) is cheap vs. expensive — an input to industrial-siting and nearshoring decisions.

sources.name: CENAGAS — SISTRANGAS (national gas pipeline system: tariffs & capacity)
sources.whatItGives: Operational and commercial data for the national integrated natural-gas transport & storage system (SISTRANGAS): published tariffs by zone, open-access capacity, and system bulletins via the electronic gestor platform. The gas-transport-infrastructure layer.
sources.accessMethod: Electronic bulletin/gestor portal (tables, DOF-published tariff PDFs, downloads)
sources.terms: Public CENAGAS regulated-service disclosures; free reuse with attribution. Mostly portal tables + PDFs — scrape/parse, less structured than the price feeds.
sources.whyItMatters: Gas-pipeline capacity and tariffs are the physical-infrastructure bottleneck for delivering gas to industrial zones — core to the nearshoring/grid-dealbreaker thesis.

sources.name: CFE — Open Data (consumption & users by municipality/tariff)
sources.whatItGives: State-utility monthly files: electricity consumption and user counts broken down by municipality and tariff class (domestic + industrial tariffs GDMTH, DIST, DIT, etc.), plus CFE's institutional data inventory. Demand-side + retail-tariff granularity.
sources.accessMethod: CSV downloads via datos.gob.mx; internal generation API endpoint
sources.endpoint: https://www.datos.gob.mx/organization/cfe  (data-opening plan https://www.datos.gob.mx/dataset/plan_apertura_datos_cfe ; generation API root https://www.cfe.gob.mx/generacion/_api/)
sources.terms: CFE open data per its published Plan de Apertura de Datos; free reuse with attribution. Best accessed via datos.gob.mx CKAN resource CSVs.
sources.whyItMatters: Municipality-level consumption + tariff data shows regional electrification and industrial demand growth — a granular nearshoring/infrastructure signal not in any macro feed.

sources.name: datos.gob.mx CKAN API (energy-datasets connector)
sources.whatItGives: The national open-data portal's still-live CKAN API — the single programmatic doorway to 400+ energy-category datasets from CFE, PEMEX, CNH, CRE/CNE, SENER as structured JSON, including datastore_search (paged rows) and datastore_search_sql (SQL over a resource).
keyIndicators: Programmatic access to any published energy resource_id
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON), CKAN action API
sources.terms: Government open data, free reuse with attribution to the publishing agency. Note: the portal front-end migrated to 'Ajolote' but the CKAN api/3 endpoints still return JSON — verify a resource_id is DataStore-backed (not just a file) before wiring datastore_search.
sources.whyItMatters: A force-multiplier connector: rather than one scraper per agency, many Mexican energy tables are reachable as clean JSON here — the fastest path to breadth, which is the whole differentiator.

notes: All sources verified reachable July 2026. Priority spine: IMSS Datos Abiertos (formal employment + registered wages, the single richest labor file) + INEGI Indicadores API v2.0 (one free token unlocks ENOE headline series, productivity/EMIM/EMEC/ENEC, all as clean JSON — wire the token once, reuse across every INEGI labor series) + CONASAMI (minimum wage). The INEGI Indicadores API is the highest-leverage wire: most INEGI labor programs below (ENOE headline, EMIM, IPL/ICUMO, EMEC, ENEC) publish their key aggregates as BIE series retrievable via that same endpoint with just the indicator code swapped — so connectors 2, 5, 6, 10, 11 can share one connector + token and differ only by indicator-code list. Data México employment (source 12) DUPLICATES what is already wired. INEGI ENOE microdata (source 4) is the only heavy connector (bulk file download, quarterly) — use it only for cuts the BIE API doesn't pre-aggregate (e.g., wages by state × sector × sex). Cadence anchors: IMSS ~day 7-9 monthly; ENOE headline monthly + full quarterly; CONASAMI annual (Dec announce, Jan 1 effective); productivity/EMIM/EMEC quarterly-to-monthly. Terms: all are Mexican federal open-government data (INEGI Ley del SNIEG / gob.mx Términos de Libre Uso MX) — free to reuse with attribution to the issuing agency; no copyrighted content, satisfies honesty laws (official, dated, one tap to primary source).

sources.name: IMSS Datos Abiertos — Asegurados / Puestos de trabajo y Salario Base de Cotización
sources.whatItGives: Monthly administrative census of the formal labor market: registered jobs (puestos permanentes/eventuales, urban/rural), insured workers, and registered contribution wage (salario base de cotización), broken down by registration delegation-subdelegation (state-level), economic sector/activity, firm size, sex, age range, and wage range (in multiples of minimum wage/UMA). This is the single richest, most timely labor dataset in Mexico.
keyIndicators: Puestos de trabajo afiliados (formal employment level, ~22.7M)
Salario base de cotización promedio (registered daily wage)
Employment by economic sector and state
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON/XML/RDF bulk download (one file per year-month, series from 1997)
sources.cadence: monthly (headline bulletin ~day 7-9; open-data files updated monthly)
sources.whyItMatters: The definitive real-time pulse of formal employment and formal wages in Mexico — moves markets and is quoted monthly. Nearline (day ~8) and far more granular than survey data. Core spine source for the labor domain.
sources.terms: Mexican open government data (aggregated only, non-nominative per Art. 22 Ley del Seguro Social); free reuse with attribution to IMSS.

sources.name: INEGI Indicadores API v2.0 (BIE) — ENOE labor headline series
sources.whatItGives: Programmatic JSON/XML access to INEGI's Banco de Información Económica time series, including the ENOE (Encuesta Nacional de Ocupación y Empleo) monthly/quarterly labor headline indicators: unemployment rate, labor participation, informality rate, underemployment, IOE (Indicadores Oportunos de Ocupación y Empleo). One free token unlocks ALL INEGI economic series by swapping the indicator code — the highest-leverage single wire in this domain.
keyIndicators: Tasa de condiciones críticas de ocupación
sources.accessMethod: REST API, JSON/JSONP/XML
sources.whyItMatters: Clean primary REST feed for the core labor-market indicators; the same connector + token is reusable for productivity, EMIM, EMEC and every other INEGI economic series — build once, reuse across the domain.
sources.terms: INEGI open data (Ley del SNIEG); free reuse with attribution to INEGI.

sources.name: CONASAMI — Salarios Mínimos Generales y Profesionales
sources.whatItGives: Official minimum wage schedule: general minimum wage and ~60 professional-occupation minimum wages, split by geographic area (Zona Libre de la Frontera Norte vs. rest of the country), plus the long historical series (general minimum wage back to 1934; general+professional 1966→present).
keyIndicators: Minimum wage — Zona Libre Frontera Norte (border zone)
sources.accessMethod: CSV download (datos.gob.mx) + published tables (gob.mx/conasami)
sources.cadence: annual (announced December, effective January 1; occasional mid-year revisions)
sources.whyItMatters: Minimum wage is a top political-economy variable in Mexico (doubled under recent policy, drives the border-zone wage floor and USMCA labor debates). The ZLFN vs rest-of-country split is a Mexico-specific detail outsiders miss.
sources.terms: Mexican open government data (Términos de Libre Uso MX); free reuse with attribution to CONASAMI.

sources.name: INEGI ENOE — Microdatos (Encuesta Nacional de Ocupación y Empleo)
sources.whatItGives: Full quarterly survey microdata (household + person records) for custom labor cuts the headline API doesn't pre-aggregate: employment, unemployment, informality, underemployment, hours worked, and earned income (ingreso laboral) by state × sector × sex × education. The authoritative labor-force survey (joint INEGI-STPS), representative at national and state level.
sources.accessMethod: Bulk file download (descarga masiva), CSV/DBF, quarterly datasets
sources.whyItMatters: The only source for state × sector wage and informality cuts and for anything below the pre-baked BIE aggregates — powers the geographic (city/state) labor detail that differentiates the brief.
sources.terms: INEGI open data (Ley del SNIEG); free reuse with attribution to INEGI.

sources.name: INEGI EMIM — Encuesta Mensual de la Industria Manufacturera
sources.whatItGives: Monthly manufacturing-sector labor and cost data: employed personnel (dependent/non-dependent), hours worked, and remuneraciones paid, across 206 SCIAN manufacturing classes (sectors 31-33). Retrievable both as BIE series (via the Indicadores API) and as press-release/tabulado files.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (INEGI Indicadores BIE, same token) + PDF/tabulado bulletins
sources.whyItMatters: Manufacturing is the nearshoring/export engine of Mexico — factory employment, hours and real wages here are the leading read on the sector most exposed to US demand and tariffs.
sources.terms: INEGI open data (Ley del SNIEG); free reuse with attribution to INEGI.

sources.name: INEGI IPL / ICUMO / IGPLE — Productividad Laboral y Costo Unitario de la Mano de Obra
sources.whatItGives: Labor productivity and unit labor cost indices: the Índice Global de Productividad Laboral de la Economía (IGPLE, based on hours worked) plus IPL (productivity) and ICUMO (unit labor cost) and real average remunerations by economic sector — manufacturing by subsector/branch AND by federal entity, trade, construction, and private non-financial services.
keyIndicators: Índice de Productividad Laboral by sector
Índice de Costo Unitario de la Mano de Obra (ICUMO)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (INEGI Indicadores BIE, same token) + interactive tabulados
sources.whyItMatters: Productivity + unit labor cost is the competitiveness variable behind the nearshoring thesis — whether Mexican wage gains are outpacing or trailing output. Directly relevant to investment/relocation decisions.
sources.terms: INEGI open data (Ley del SNIEG); free reuse with attribution to INEGI.

sources.name: STPS — Estadísticas del Sector Laboral (ENOE tabulados) & Observatorio Laboral (OLA)
sources.whatItGives: STPS's curated labor tabulados derived from ENOE + administrative records from the Servicio Nacional de Empleo (SNE): monthly and quarterly occupation/employment/formality indicators disaggregated by state, plus the Observatorio Laboral portal with wages, employment and vacancy data by occupation/university career.
sources.accessMethod: CSV via datos.gob.mx + STPS HTML statistical tables + OLA portal
sources.whyItMatters: Adds the occupation-level and vacancy angle (labor demand) and pre-packaged state cuts the ministry itself publishes — complements the INEGI supply-side view with STPS's policy framing.
sources.terms: Mexican open government data (Términos de Libre Uso MX); free reuse with attribution to STPS.

sources.name: INEGI EMEC — Encuesta Mensual sobre Empresas Comerciales
