sources.whatItGives: Monthly employment and remuneration for the wholesale and retail trade sector: employed personnel and real remunerations index by trade subsector — the labor read on Mexico's largest employer sector after informal/self-employment.
keyIndicators: Personal ocupado — comercio al por mayor y menor
sources.accessMethod: REST API (INEGI Indicadores BIE, same token) + tabulados/bulletins
sources.whyItMatters: Retail/wholesale trade is a massive share of formal employment and a consumption-demand signal; wages here track domestic purchasing power and remittance-fueled consumption.
sources.terms: INEGI open data (Ley del SNIEG); free reuse with attribution to INEGI.

sources.name: INEGI ENEC — Encuesta Nacional de Empresas Constructoras
sources.whatItGives: Monthly construction-sector labor data: employed personnel (obreros/empleados), hours worked, and real remunerations, national and by federal entity — the labor pulse of infrastructure and real-estate activity.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (INEGI Indicadores BIE, same token) + tabulados/bulletins
sources.whyItMatters: Construction employment is a fast, pro-cyclical read on public infrastructure spend and private investment — relevant to the nearshoring build-out and government capex story.
sources.terms: INEGI open data (Ley del SNIEG); free reuse with attribution to INEGI.

sources.name: Data México (Secretaría de Economía) — Employment / Empleo cubes
sources.whatItGives: OLAP-queryable employment indicators (IMSS-derived formal employment and ENOE-derived labor stats) by state, municipality and industry, pre-joined to the economic-complexity and trade cubes.
sources.accessMethod: OLAP / Tesseract REST API (JSON)
sources.whyItMatters: Convenient pre-aggregated cuts, but the underlying data is IMSS + ENOE which we should wire at the primary source for freshness and honesty-law traceability.
sources.terms: Mexican open government data; free reuse with attribution to Secretaría de Economía.

notes: Banxico SIE is already wired as a source, but it is a giant catalog: the FDI/financial-account, foreign-holdings-of-Mbonos, and remittances-by-state/municipality series below are DISTINCT data feeds reachable through the same already-wired API/token — cheap to add, so flagged duplicatesExisting=true only in the "already have the pipe" sense. The genuinely new pipes here are: Secretaría de Economía RNIE (the authoritative FDI-by-country/sector/state source — NOT currently wired), OECD FDI BMD4 SDMX, IMF CDIS/BOP-IIP SDMX, and US BEA Direct Investment (US-side bilateral). fDi Markets (greenfield announcements) is the one high-value source that is paid with no free API — flagged hard. All Banxico series IDs must be resolved from the catalogoSeries endpoint before wiring (idCuadro codes given are the SIE table anchors; each table expands to SF/SG series IDs the API consumes). Recommend one shared Banxico connector that fans out over a series-ID map rather than N connectors.

sources.name: Secretaría de Economía — RNIE Foreign Direct Investment statistics (quarterly)
sources.whatItGives: The authoritative Mexican FDI dataset: inward FDI flows by country of origin, by economic sector/subsector, by federal entity (state), and by type (new investment / reinvested earnings / intercompany accounts). Quarterly report + full historical series since 1999 (sector/state since 1980/1989). This is the primary RNIE source Alan named.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download (datos.gob.mx dataset resources) + companion Excel workbooks ('carpeta IED' .xlsm) on economia.gob.mx
sources.endpoint: https://www.datos.gob.mx/dataset/inversion_extranjera_directa (dataset landing; resource CSVs linked). Excel workbook pattern: https://www.economia.gob.mx/files/gobmx/ied/carpeta_ied_<YYYY><Q>T.xlsm . Program page: https://www.gob.mx/se/acciones-y-programas/competitividad-y-normatividad-inversion-extranjera-directa
sources.cadence: Quarterly (release ~45-60 days after quarter close; series are revised retroactively — re-pull full history each refresh)
sources.terms: Mexican federal open data (Términos de Libre Uso MX / datos.gob.mx). Free reuse with attribution to Secretaría de Economía. Primary + dated.
sources.whyItMatters: The single most important capital-flows source for Mexico and the headline the whole 'nearshoring' narrative rests on. Nothing already wired provides FDI broken down by state/country/sector — Data Mexico is trade/employment, World Bank is aggregate only.

sources.name: Banxico SIE — Balance of Payments: financial account & Direct Investment (BPM6, directional principle)
sources.whatItGives: Quarterly balance-of-payments financial account: direct investment (inward/outward, directional principle), portfolio investment, other investment, and the current/capital account context. The macro counterpart to the SE micro FDI data, on IMF BPM6 basis.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (Banxico SIE API v1) — same API already wired for FX/CPI
sources.endpoint: https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieAPIRest/service/v1/series/{idSeries}/datos — resolve IDs from SIE tables CE131 (Inversión Directa de México, principio direccional) and CE174; catalog: https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieAPIRest/service/v1/doc/catalogoSeries
sources.terms: Banxico SIE terms of use — free programmatic access with token; attribution to Banco de México. Primary + dated.
sources.whyItMatters: Gives the macro/BPM6 view of FDI + portfolio flows that reconciles against SE's registry data, and is the only source for net OUTWARD Mexican investment and the full financial account.

sources.name: Banxico SIE — Foreign holdings of government securities (tenencia de valores gubernamentales, incl. Mbonos)
sources.whatItGives: Holdings of Mexican federal government securities by holder sector, isolating non-resident/foreign holdings, by instrument (Mbonos, Cetes, Bondes, Udibonos). The 'hot money' / foreign appetite gauge Alan named. Weekly-frequency series.
keyIndicators: foreign holdings of Mbonos (MXN & % of outstanding)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (Banxico SIE API v1)
sources.endpoint: https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieAPIRest/service/v1/series/{idSeries}/datos — resolve IDs from SIE sector 7 tables CF457 and CA175 (Tenencia de valores gubernamentales por sector tenedor). Human view: https://www.banxico.org.mx/mercados/valores-gubernamentales-secto.html
sources.terms: Banxico SIE terms — free with token; attribute Banco de México. Primary + dated.
sources.whyItMatters: The most sensitive, highest-frequency capital-flow signal Mexico publishes — foreign positioning in local debt moves on peso/rate risk well before quarterly BoP. Directly answers Alan's 'foreign holdings of Mbonos' ask; not currently wired.

sources.name: Banxico SIE — Remittances by state and by municipality (detail)
sources.whatItGives: Remittance INFLOWS disaggregated by receiving federal entity (CA79) and by municipality (CE166) — the corridor/geography detail. Banxico's headline national remittances number is likely already wired; this is the sub-national breakdown.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (Banxico SIE API v1) — note ~100-series-per-call cap; municipal pull must be batched
sources.endpoint: https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieAPIRest/service/v1/series/{idSeries}/datos — tables CA79 (por entidad federativa) and CE166 (distribución por municipio)
sources.terms: Banxico SIE terms — free with token; attribute Banco de México. Primary + dated.
sources.whyItMatters: Remittances are ~4% of GDP and the dominant household capital inflow; the state/municipal cut ties directly to migration and local-economy stories and is a genuine differentiator vs the single national headline.

sources.name: CNBV — Portafolio de Información (bank credit, deposits, financial soundness)
sources.whatItGives: Regulator's full statistical portfolio for supervised institutions: commercial-bank credit portfolio by segment (commercial/consumer/mortgage), NPL ratios, deposits, capitalization (ICAP), profitability, branches — monthly. Plus Financial Inclusion databases at state/municipal level.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/Excel download from the Portafolio BI portal (per-dataset files); Financial Inclusion as bulk DB downloads. No documented REST API — stable file-download / scrape.
sources.endpoint: https://portafolioinfo.cnbv.gob.mx/ (Portafolio de Información BI). Financial inclusion: https://www.gob.mx/cnbv/acciones-y-programas/bases-de-datos-de-inclusion-financiera
sources.terms: Mexican open government data; free reuse with attribution to CNBV. Primary + dated. (Download engine prefers direct file links; watch for portal changes.)
sources.whyItMatters: Domestic credit is the internal capital-formation counterpart to foreign flows — bank lending, NPLs and capital ratios are the health check on whether external capital is being intermediated. No banking-sector source is currently wired.

sources.name: OECD — FDI statistics (Benchmark Definition 4th ed., BMD4) via SDMX API
sources.whatItGives: Standardized FDI on OECD/IMF BMD4 basis for Mexico: main aggregates (inward/outward flows, positions, income), FDI by counterpart area (partner country), and FDI by economic activity. Internationally comparable — lets you benchmark Mexico vs peers on identical methodology.
sources.accessMethod: REST API — SDMX-JSON / SDMX-ML (OECD Data Explorer / SDMX endpoint)
sources.endpoint: Dataflows DSD_FDI@DF_FDI_AGGR_SUMM (main aggregates), DF_FDI_FLOW_CTRY (by counterpart), DF_FDI_FLOW_IND (by activity), agency OECD.DAF.INV. Explorer: https://data-explorer.oecd.org/ ; SDMX docs: https://data.oecd.org/api/sdmx-json-documentation/
sources.terms: OECD terms of use — free reuse with attribution/source citation for most datasets. Primary + dated.
sources.whyItMatters: The apples-to-apples benchmark layer: compare Mexico's FDI to other OECD/emerging peers on one methodology, and get partner-country breakdowns that reconcile against SE's registry. Standardized comparability is exactly the differentiator for outsider readers.

sources.name: IMF — Coordinated Direct Investment Survey (CDIS) + BOP/IIP via IMF Data SDMX API
sources.whatItGives: Bilateral inward and outward direct investment POSITIONS (Mexico vis-à-vis every partner economy) from CDIS, plus full Balance of Payments and International Investment Position datasets on BPM6. Mirror data lets you see who invests in Mexico and where Mexico invests, cross-checkable against counterparties.
sources.accessMethod: REST API — SDMX 2.1 / SDMX 3.0 (IMF Data / SDMX Central)
sources.endpoint: SDMX 2.1 base: https://sdmxcentral.imf.org/ws/public/sdmxapi/rest ; SDMX 3.0 base: https://sdmxcentral.imf.org/sdmx/v2 ; portal https://data.imf.org/ (datasets: CDIS, BOP, IIP). Mexico country code MX/273.
sources.terms: IMF data — free use with attribution; standard IMF terms. Primary + dated.
sources.whyItMatters: Only source giving bilateral FDI POSITIONS (stocks) with mirror-data validation, and the internationally-standardized IIP that shows Mexico's overall external asset/liability position. Complements OECD flows with stocks.

sources.name: US BEA — Direct Investment by Country and Industry (US↔Mexico bilateral)
sources.whatItGives: US-side bilateral direct investment: US FDI position and flows INTO Mexico, and Mexican FDI into the US, by industry and on historical-cost/UBO basis; plus the International Transactions Accounts. The US-side data that moves Mexico, from the largest single investor country.
keyIndicators: US direct investment position in Mexico
sources.accessMethod: REST API (BEA Data API, JSON/XML; free registration key). Datasets: MNE (Direct Investment & Multinational Enterprises), ITA (International Transactions).
sources.endpoint: https://apps.bea.gov/api/data (API); key signup https://apps.bea.gov/API/signup/ ; dataset docs https://www.bea.gov/data/intl-trade-investment/direct-investment-country-and-industry
sources.terms: US federal public-domain data; free reuse, attribute BEA. Primary + dated.
sources.whyItMatters: The US is Mexico's dominant FDI source; BEA gives the counterparty's own high-quality read on that bilateral relationship, independent of Mexican reporting — a cross-check and an early US-policy signal.

sources.name: fDi Markets (Financial Times / fDi Intelligence) — greenfield FDI announcements tracker
sources.whatItGives: Real-time database of announced greenfield/cross-border investment PROJECTS (company, source country, destination state, sector, capex, jobs) — the forward-looking leading indicator that precedes realized FDI in official stats. The named greenfield-announcement tracker.
sources.accessMethod: Proprietary database / data licensing; no public free API. Access via paid subscription (possible enterprise data feed/licensing on request).
sources.terms: Commercial license required; NOT free, NOT redistributable without agreement — would violate the free/citable bar unless licensed. Cannot republish underlying records.
sources.whyItMatters: The only leading indicator of FDI (announcements lead realized flows by 12-24 months) and the backbone of nearshoring coverage. Flagged because it fails the free/wireable bar — evaluate a licensed feed later; for now, use official realized-FDI sources above.

sources.name: Banxico SIE — Portfolio investment flows (inversión de cartera, financial account)
sources.whatItGives: The portfolio-investment component of the financial account: non-resident purchases/sales of Mexican equity and debt securities (and resident holdings abroad). Complements the foreign-holdings STOCK series with FLOW data.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (Banxico SIE API v1)
sources.endpoint: https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieAPIRest/service/v1/series/{idSeries}/datos — resolve from BoP financial-account tables (sector 1; see CA111 summary and CE131/CE174 structures)
sources.terms: Banxico SIE terms — free with token; attribute Banco de México. Primary + dated.
sources.whyItMatters: Separates 'hot' portfolio capital from 'sticky' FDI — the two behave very differently under stress, and having both flow and stock (Mbonos holdings) views gives a complete capital-flows picture.

notes: Verified all endpoints live July 2026. Key wireability facts: (1) The INEGI Indicadores API is the single most valuable wire here — one REST endpoint + free token unlocks INPC (detailed CPI baskets), INPP, consumer confidence (ENCO), EMEC retail, and hundreds of other series by swapping the indicator ID. This is DISTINCT from what's already wired: you have Banxico SIE (which mirrors some INEGI series) and Data Mexico OLAP, but NOT INEGI's own Indicadores API, which is the primary source for granular CPI basket detail. Strongly recommend wiring INEGI Indicadores as a first-class connector. (2) Banxico SIE is already wired — the consumer-credit / credit-card CAT SIE cuadros (CF303, CF870, etc.) are reachable through your EXISTING SIE token, so they're near-zero marginal cost; flagged as duplicative access-path. (3) The Banxico Comparador Crediticio API is a SEPARATE Banxico system from SIE (different base URL, needs its own session token, POST-based) — product-level CAT/rates for credit cards, payroll, auto, mortgage loans; not a SIE duplicate. (4) ANTAD publishes only HTML tables/PDF — no CSV/API — so it needs a stable scrape of antad.net/indicadores. (5) CNBV has two distinct assets: the Portafolio de Información (institution-level financial/operational statements incl. IFPE fintech entities) and the Bases de Datos de Inclusión Financiera (quarterly infra + product-holding + fintech usage); both are bulk CSV/Excel downloads, no REST API. (6) ENIGH is biennial microdata (CSV bulk download) — heavy but the authoritative household income/spending source. All sources are official/primary, free, and citable with dated releases. INEGI content is open data (Términos de Libre Uso de la Información del INEGI — attribution). Recommend wiring priority: INEGI Indicadores API > CNBV Inclusión Financiera > Comparador Crediticio > CNBV Portafolio > EMEC (via API) > ANTAD scrape > ENIGH.

sources.name: INEGI Indicadores API — INPC detailed CPI baskets
sources.whatItGives: National Consumer Price Index (INPC) at full granularity: headline, core/non-core, and the detailed sub-indices/baskets (food, energy, services, merchandise, housing, etc.), plus biweekly (quincenal) prints. One REST endpoint returns any indicator's full historical series by ID.
keyIndicators: CPI by object-of-spending division (food & beverages, transport, housing, etc.)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/JSONP/XML)
sources.whyItMatters: The single highest-value wire in this domain: the authoritative, primary CPI source with basket-level detail you cannot get from Banxico SIE. One connector + token pattern scales to every INEGI series (see next rows).
sources.terms: INEGI open data — Términos de Libre Uso de la Información del INEGI (free reuse with attribution)

sources.name: INEGI Indicadores API — INPP (Producer Price Index)
sources.whatItGives: National Producer Price Index: price variations of goods & services produced domestically, by economic sector/activity and for construction (used for cost-escalation). Same API, different indicator IDs. Base July 2019=100.
keyIndicators: INPP by sector of economic activity
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/JSONP/XML)
sources.whyItMatters: Upstream cost pressure — leads consumer prices and margins; sector detail flags where inflation is building before it reaches shelves. Reuses the INEGI connector already needed for INPC.
sources.terms: INEGI open data — Términos de Libre Uso de la Información del INEGI

sources.name: INEGI ENCO — Consumer Confidence (via Indicadores API / BIE)
sources.whatItGives: Encuesta Nacional sobre Confianza del Consumidor: the Consumer Confidence Index (ICC) and its five components (household economic situation now vs a year ago, country situation, willingness to buy durable goods), seasonally-adjusted and original. Joint INEGI–Banxico survey, exposed as BIE series.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/JSONP/XML) via INEGI BIE
sources.whyItMatters: The leading soft indicator for consumer spending and sentiment; moves with FX/inflation shocks and elections. Same connector as INPC/INPP. NOTE: also mirrored in Banxico SIE (cuadro CR354) which you already have wired — pick one source.
sources.terms: INEGI open data — Términos de Libre Uso

sources.name: INEGI EMEC — Monthly Commercial Establishments Survey (retail & wholesale)
sources.whatItGives: Encuesta Mensual sobre Empresas Comerciales: real income (sales) indices for retail (al por menor) and wholesale (al por mayor) commerce, plus employed personnel and real remuneration. The official domestic-commerce activity gauge. Base 2018=100.
keyIndicators: Real retail sales index (ingresos reales al por menor)
Real wholesale sales index (al por mayor)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/JSONP/XML) via INEGI BIE; also monthly PDF boletines
sources.whyItMatters: Official, economy-wide retail-sales pulse (broader and more citable than ANTAD's members-only universe). Same INEGI connector.
sources.terms: INEGI open data — Términos de Libre Uso

sources.name: ANTAD — Retail Sales Indicator (same-store & total)
sources.whatItGives: Asociación Nacional de Tiendas de Autoservicio y Departamentales monthly release: same-store sales growth (VMT / tiendas iguales) and total sales growth (tiendas totales) for supermarkets, department stores, and specialty chains, plus peso sales levels. Higher-frequency, private-sector read on organized retail.
sources.accessMethod: Scrape (HTML table + monthly press release; no CSV/API)
sources.whyItMatters: Earliest monthly signal on organized-retail consumer demand — released weeks before INEGI EMEC. Needs a stable scrape and careful attribution (private association data).
sources.terms: Private association site; general Términos y Condiciones — cite ANTAD, link-out, do not republish tables wholesale

sources.name: CNBV — Bases de Datos de Inclusión Financiera
sources.whatItGives: Quarterly financial-inclusion databases: infrastructure (branches, ATMs, POS terminals, banking agents) and product holdings (deposit/savings accounts, credit cards, consumer/payroll/mortgage loans, mobile-linked accounts & transactions) disaggregated to state and municipality, including fintech (IFPE) usage. Downloadable data tables.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/Excel bulk download
sources.whyItMatters: The authoritative supply-side map of banking + fintech penetration in Mexico — directly answers the fintech-adoption question with geographic granularity. Static-file download fits the pre-baked-JSON model well.
sources.terms: CNBV / gob.mx open government data — free reuse with attribution

sources.name: CNBV — Portafolio de Información (institution financial statements)
sources.whatItGives: Periodic financial and operational statistics for every CNBV-supervised entity: commercial banks, sofipos/socaps (popular finance), brokerage houses, and fintech ITFs (crowdfunding + IFPE electronic-payment-fund institutions). Balance-sheet, income, portfolio-quality (NPLs), and consumer-credit stock/rates by institution.
keyIndicators: Consumer & credit-card loan portfolio by bank
sources.accessMethod: CSV/Excel download via BI portal (consolidated Dec-2023)
sources.whyItMatters: Institution-level banking + fintech health and consumer-credit stock — the primary source for credit growth, delinquency, and fintech scale. Portal is BI-driven so wiring means hitting its export/download endpoints (medium effort).
sources.terms: CNBV / gob.mx open government data — free reuse with attribution

sources.name: Banxico — Comparador Crediticio API (product-level CAT & rates)
sources.whatItGives: Segmented, product-level cost data pulled from regulated institutions' Catálogo Único de Productos: CAT (all-in APR), interest rates, commissions, and terms for credit cards, payroll loans, auto loans, and mortgages — by institution and credit-limit segment.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (POST, JSON) — separate Banxico system from SIE
sources.auth: free session token (own token, distinct from SIE token)
sources.cadence: Updated as institutions refile CUP (roughly monthly)
sources.whyItMatters: The real cost of consumer credit at product granularity — powers 'what Mexicans actually pay to borrow.' Distinct from SIE aggregate CAT series; POST + session-token flow makes it medium effort.
sources.terms: Banxico public API — free use, cite Banco de México

sources.name: Banxico SIE — Consumer credit & credit-card CAT aggregate series
sources.whatItGives: Aggregate consumer-credit interest-rate / CAT series and consumer credit stock: revolving-card CAT (CF870), household-credit CAT (CF303), consumer-credit balances and rates. Standard SIE time-series.
keyIndicators: Tasas de crédito a los hogares
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON) — SIE API v1
sources.auth: free token — SAME token already wired for FX/CPI/rate
sources.whyItMatters: Near-zero marginal cost: reachable through the Banxico SIE connector you already run — just add the consumer-credit series IDs. Good aggregate complement to the product-level Comparador API.
sources.terms: Banxico public API — free use, cite Banco de México

sources.name: INEGI ENIGH — Household Income & Expenditure Survey (microdata)
sources.whatItGives: Encuesta Nacional de Ingresos y Gastos de los Hogares: authoritative household income (by source) and expenditure (by category) with full distribution — deciles, average/median income, spending shares on food/housing/transport/health, and non-monetary income. Microdata for custom cuts.
keyIndicators: Expenditure share by category (food, housing, transport…)
Average current monetary income per household
sources.accessMethod: CSV/DBF microdata bulk download (also tabulados via Tableros Estadísticos)
sources.whyItMatters: The ground-truth on what Mexican households earn and spend — the denominator behind every consumer/fintech story. Heavy microdata + biennial cadence make it a batch-processed reference rather than a live tick, hence 'hard'.
sources.terms: INEGI open data — Términos de Libre Uso de la Información del INEGI

notes: Verified reachable endpoints via WebFetch/WebSearch. Two SHCP pages failed to fetch on TLS cert verification (unable to verify first certificate) but are confirmed live via search + are the canonical government portals; a Node connector should pass a relaxed/updated CA bundle or use the presto.hacienda.gob.mx open-data endpoint. DUPLICATE FLAGS: World Bank WEO/structural indicators already wired — did NOT add. Banxico SIE core (FX, CPI, policy rate, remittances) already wired; the Survey of Professional Forecasters (Encuesta de Especialistas) rides the SAME SIE API + token you already hold but exposes NEW forecast series (consensus GDP/inflation/FX/rate expectations) — additive, near-zero wiring cost. INEGI BIE is the single biggest unlock here (IGAE monthly, PIB quarterly, flash GDP, national accounts) and is NOT yet wired. IMF WEO overlaps World Bank on a few headline series but adds 5-yr projections + fiscal balance/debt forecasts + the twice-yearly IMF view — keep it. OECD adds Composite Leading Indicators (turning-point signal) not available elsewhere. Recommend wiring order: (1) INEGI BIE, (2) SHCP Estadísticas Oportunas, (3) Banxico Survey series, (4) IMF WEO, (5) OECD, (6) datos.gob.mx/Transparencia as catalog fallback.

sources.name: INEGI — Banco de Indicadores API (BIE / BISE)
sources.whatItGives: Programmatic access to INEGI's full economic indicator catalog: IGAE (monthly economic activity), PIB quarterly + flash GDP estimate, national accounts (supply/demand, by activity), industrial production, ENOE-derived series, prices, and thousands more, national/state/municipal, with full historical series and the exact release timestamp.
keyIndicators: Fixed investment & private consumption monthly indicators
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON / JSONP / XML / JSON-Stat / PC-Axis)
sources.auth: free token (register once on INEGI site)
sources.cadence: per-series; IGAE monthly, PIB quarterly, flash GDP ~30 days after quarter — API returns each series' true update time
sources.whyItMatters: The spine of Mexican real-activity data and the single largest gap in the current stack — nothing wired today gives monthly IGAE or quarterly PIB straight from the primary source. One free token unlocks hundreds of series with the same connector pattern.
sources.terms: Free public use; INEGI open-data terms, attribution to INEGI. Data is official/primary and dated.

sources.name: SHCP — Estadísticas Oportunas de Finanzas Públicas (open-data DB)
sources.whatItGives: The authoritative monthly public-finances dataset: government revenue (tax/oil/non-tax), expenditure (programmable/non-programmable), the public-sector balance (RFSP / primary balance), financing, and federal transfers to states/municipalities — for federal govt, non-financial & financial state enterprises, and the consolidated public sector.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/Excel download + query interface (Datos Abiertos base de datos); scriptable via the ESTOPOR base-datos endpoint
sources.cadence: monthly (release calendar published; typically ~30 days after month-end)
sources.whyItMatters: There is NO fiscal/government-finance source wired today — this is the core gap for the fiscal half of the domain. Primary source, dated, downloadable. Note: government host may present a TLS chain the connector must trust (ship an updated CA bundle).
sources.terms: Public government data, datos abiertos (Libre Uso MX / open licence); attribute SHCP.

sources.name: SHCP — SHRFSP (Saldo Histórico de los RFSP, broadest public-debt measure)
sources.whatItGives: The historical balance of public-sector financial requirements — Mexico's broadest and most-cited debt aggregate (the '% of GDP' headline), plus its components (domestic vs external, government vs enterprises). Published alongside the Estadísticas Oportunas as 'Requerimientos Financieros y su Saldo Histórico'.
keyIndicators: Debt of federal govt vs public enterprises
sources.accessMethod: CSV/Excel download (same ESTOPOR open-data DB, separate table + metadata PDF)
sources.endpoint: https://www.secciones.hacienda.gob.mx/es/estadisticas_oportunas/base_de_datos (section: Requerimientos Financieros y su Saldo Histórico) — mirror: http://presto.hacienda.gob.mx/EstoporLayout/
sources.whyItMatters: The debt-to-GDP number every analyst quotes for Mexico, straight from the issuer. No debt series currently wired. Same connector as the fiscal balance dataset, so marginal cost is low once ESTOPOR is integrated.
sources.terms: datos abiertos, attribute SHCP.

sources.name: Banxico — Survey of Professional Forecasters (Encuesta de Especialistas) via SIE API
sources.whatItGives: Monthly consensus private-sector forecasts: expected GDP growth, headline & core inflation (year-end and 12m), USD/MXN, the Banxico policy rate path, fiscal deficit and public-debt expectations, plus qualitative 'business climate' balances. Aggregate historical series live in the SIE and are pullable by series ID.
keyIndicators: Consensus GDP growth forecast (current & next year)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (Banxico SIE, JSON) — same API/token already wired
sources.auth: free token (the Banxico SIE token you already hold)
sources.cadence: monthly (survey published first business days of each month; 2026 calendar published)
sources.whyItMatters: Adds the forward-looking / expectations layer the stack lacks — you have realized macro but no consensus view. Rides the ALREADY-WIRED SIE connector and token: only new series IDs to add, near-zero integration cost.
sources.terms: Banxico open data; free use with attribution.

sources.name: IMF — World Economic Outlook (WEO) via SDMX 3.0 API
sources.whatItGives: IMF's macro database + 5-year projections for Mexico: real GDP growth, inflation, unemployment, current account, general-government fiscal balance and gross debt, output gap — with the twice-yearly IMF house view and revisions.
sources.accessMethod: SDMX 3.0 REST API (SDMX-JSON); also bulk WEO flatfile download twice a year
sources.endpoint: https://api.imf.org/external/sdmx/3.0 — catalog: /structure/dataflow ; data: /data/{dataflow}/{key}?... ; dataset page: https://data.imf.org/en/datasets/IMF.RES:WEO ; API guide: https://data.imf.org/en/Resource-Pages/IMF-API
sources.cadence: WEO updated 2x/year (April & October) + WEO Update; API otherwise live
sources.whyItMatters: Adds authoritative multilateral projections and IMF-view fiscal/debt forecasts. Overlaps World Bank on a couple of realized headline series (flag), but the 5-yr forecast path and general-government fiscal/debt series are not duplicated by anything wired.
sources.terms: IMF open data terms; free with attribution. Note SDMX codelists must be resolved from the DSD, not hardcoded.

sources.name: OECD — Data API (SDMX-JSON), Mexico + Economic Outlook & CLI
sources.whatItGives: OECD's harmonized cross-country series for Mexico: OECD Economic Outlook projections, Composite Leading Indicators (turning-point signal), Main Economic Indicators, quarterly national accounts, harmonized unemployment, government deficit/debt (SNA basis), productivity.
keyIndicators: Composite Leading Indicator (CLI) — cycle turning points
OECD Economic Outlook GDP & inflation projections
sources.accessMethod: REST API, SDMX-JSON (append ?format=jsondata)
sources.cadence: per dataset; CLI monthly, Economic Outlook 2x/year, MEI monthly
sources.whyItMatters: The Composite Leading Indicator is a genuinely differentiated, cited early-warning cycle signal not obtainable from the wired sources; OECD also gives a second independent projection view. SDMX pattern is reusable with the IMF connector.
sources.terms: Free of charge subject to OECD Terms & Conditions; attribution to OECD. Rate-limited — batch queries.

sources.name: datos.gob.mx — Plataforma Nacional de Datos Abiertos (CKAN API)
sources.whatItGives: Federal open-data catalog fronting SHCP, SAT, INEGI-linked and other agency datasets via CKAN — programmatic package search + DataStore SQL/filter queries. Useful as a discovery layer and a fallback structured endpoint for SHCP fiscal resources and SAT tax-collection open data.
sources.accessMethod: REST CKAN Action API (package_search, datastore_search, datastore_search_sql) returning JSON
sources.whyItMatters: Standard CKAN API = a single reusable connector that discovers and pulls many fiscal/tax resources, and gives a JSON alternative when a source ministry only offers Excel. Good glue, not a headline series source.
sources.terms: Libre Uso MX open licence; attribute source agency.

sources.name: SHCP — Transparencia Presupuestaria, Datos Abiertos (budget/PEF & spending)
sources.whatItGives: Budget-side complement to Estadísticas Oportunas: approved federal budget (PEF), programmable spending by ramo/program/function, budget execution vs approved, and federal-transfer distribution — the granular expenditure view behind the aggregate fiscal balance.
sources.accessMethod: CSV / Excel / JSON open-data downloads (bulk files per exercise year)
sources.whyItMatters: Turns the top-line deficit into a composition story (where money is budgeted vs spent) — no expenditure-composition source is wired. Primary SHCP data, downloadable, dated.
sources.terms: datos abiertos, Libre Uso MX; attribute SHCP.

notes:
Big structural finding: most of Mexico's markets/rates data lives inside the SAME Banxico SIE REST API you already have wired (for FX/CPI/policy-rate/remittances). Everything below tagged "SIE" is not a new connector — it is a new set of series IDs on the connector you already run, so wireability is "easy" and marginal cost is near-zero. That is a major efficiency: one auth token, one code path, adds the entire domestic rates + crude complex. SIE token: GET https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieAPIRest/service/v1/token (free, instant, 64-char; pass as Bmx-Token header or ?token=). Query: /series/{IDs}/datos/oportuno (latest) or /series/{IDs}/datos/{from}/{to} (range); comma-join up to ~20 IDs per call; JSON default. TLS 1.3 required. Series IDs below are the ones I could confirm by name; before wiring, hit /SieAPIRest/service/v1/doc/catalogoSeries (the full catalog) or the cuadro pages to lock exact IDs, since Banxico has both "primary-auction" and "secondary-market" variants of most rate series and they are easy to confuse.

Genuinely NEW connectors (not Banxico): World Bank GEM (EMBI), BCRP Peru (EMBIG Mexico daily — the cleanest free primary-market sovereign-spread feed), WorldGovernmentBonds (CDS + live curve scrape), and the equity index (S&P/BMV IPC is licensed/paid at the official source; Yahoo ^MXX is the practical free feed).

Two duplicate flags: (a) Banxico policy rate is already wired — do not re-add it; TIIE de Fondeo is a different, market-determined overnight rate and IS worth adding. (b) If your "some macro series" from SIE already includes CETES 28d, dedupe before adding the curve.

Gaps I did NOT find a clean free primary source for and would flag as "buy or scrape" decisions: official real-time IPC/Mexbol level (S&P DJI licenses it; BMV official API is entitlement-gated), and a free JPMorgan EMBI+ Mexico proper (World Bank GEM + BCRP are the free stand-ins). Everything I list resolved to a real, reachable endpoint.

sources.name: Banxico SIE — CETES primary-auction yield curve (28/91/182/364d)
sources.whatItGives: Weekly primary-auction yields for Cetes across the four standard tenors — the short end of the Mexican sovereign curve and the reference for money-market pricing.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON) — Banxico SIE
sources.endpoint: https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieAPIRest/service/v1/series/SF282,SF3338,SF3270,SF3367/datos/oportuno  (SF282=28d, SF3338=91d, SF3270=182d, SF3367=364d; verify IDs against /doc/catalogoSeries before wiring)
sources.cadence: weekly (Tuesday auction) + daily secondary variants exist
sources.whyItMatters: The single most-watched Mexican rate after the policy rate; every retail saver, treasury desk and news outlet quotes Cetes. Same connector you already run — pure series-ID addition.
sources.terms: Public data, SIE disclaimer acceptance required; cite Banco de México + date.

sources.name: Banxico SIE — TIIE (28d/91d) and TIIE de Fondeo overnight
sources.whatItGives: Interbank equilibrium rates: legacy TIIE 28/91-day (still referenced in loans) plus the market-determined overnight TIIE de Fondeo that is replacing them.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON) — Banxico SIE
sources.endpoint: https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieAPIRest/service/v1/series/SF43783/datos/oportuno  (TIIE de Fondeo overnight lives under cuadro CA684 — pull its exact SIE id from that table); RSS mirror: https://www.banxico.org.mx/rsscb/rss?BMXC_canal=tiie&BMXC_idioma=es
sources.whyItMatters: The floating-rate benchmark for Mexican credit; TIIE de Fondeo is the reform successor and a cleaner market signal than the policy rate you already carry. Not a duplicate of the policy rate.
sources.terms: Public; cite Banxico + date. RSS channel is officially published.

sources.name: Banxico SIE — Bonos M & Udibonos secondary-market yields (the long curve)
sources.whatItGives: Secondary-market yields-to-maturity for nominal fixed-rate Bonos M and inflation-linked Udibonos across nodes (3/5/10/20/30y) — the belly and long end of the curve, plus a market-implied breakeven-inflation read (Bono M minus Udibono).
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON) — Banxico SIE
sources.whyItMatters: Completes the sovereign curve beyond Cetes and gives a free, official breakeven-inflation series (Bono M vs Udibono) that markets watch as an inflation-expectations gauge. Same connector.
sources.terms: Public; cite Banxico + date.

sources.name: Banxico SIE — Mezcla Mexicana crude export price (daily)
sources.whatItGives: Daily price (USD/bbl) of the Mexican crude export blend — the fiscal keystone for Pemex revenue and the federal budget's oil-price assumption.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON) — Banxico SIE (also HTML at banxico.org.mx/apps/gc/precios-spot-del-petroleo-gra.html)
sources.endpoint: https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieAPIRest/service/v1/series/{id}/datos/oportuno  (series sits in SIE sector=25, cuadro CA357: https://www.banxico.org.mx/SieInternet/consultarDirectorioInternetAction.do?sector=25&idCuadro=CA357&accion=consultarCuadroAnalitico&locale=es — grab the SF id there)
sources.whyItMatters: Oil is the swing variable in Mexican public finances; the mezcla price moves the peso, Pemex spreads and budget math. Officially published by Banxico so it clears the honesty bar. Same connector.
sources.terms: Public, informational estimate published by Banxico/Pemex; cite + date.

sources.name: Banxico — Government securities auction RESULTS (subastas)
sources.whatItGives: Weekly primary-auction results as financial agent of the government: amount placed, weighted-average allotment rate, bid-to-cover, per instrument (Cetes, Bondes F/G, Bonos M, Udibonos) and per tenor.
sources.accessMethod: Stable scrape / structured HTML (dao-web); paired convocatorias page for the announced schedule
sources.whyItMatters: Auction demand (bid-to-cover) is a real-time read on appetite for Mexican debt that headline yields hide — a differentiated signal few briefings surface. Primary source, dated.
sources.terms: Public official results; link-out to Banxico page as citation. Scrape the table, don't republish PDFs wholesale.

sources.name: World Bank Global Economic Monitor (GEM) — EMBI Mexico spread
sources.whatItGives: EMBI (emerging-market sovereign bond) spread time series for Mexico via the World Bank GEM database — a free stand-in for the proprietary JPMorgan EMBI+.
sources.accessMethod: REST API / DataBank download — same World Bank platform family you already use
sources.endpoint: https://databank.worldbank.org/id/d79f5818?Report_Name=EMBI-TS  · programmatic via World Bank GEM indicator API
sources.whyItMatters: Sovereign spread is THE market verdict on Mexico's credit; free and citable. Note you already wire World Bank REST, so this is a familiar connector — but a distinct dataset from the GDP/structural series you pull.
sources.terms: World Bank open data / CC-BY 4.0; attribute World Bank GEM. (Underlying EMBI is JPMorgan-derived — cite as republished by World Bank.)

sources.name: BCRP (Central Bank of Peru) — EMBIG Mexico daily spread
sources.whatItGives: Daily EMBIG spread for Mexico (basis points) published in BCRPData — the cleanest free DAILY sovereign-spread series for Mexico, updated faster than World Bank GEM.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/CSV) + HTML — BCRPData series service
sources.whyItMatters: Fills the daily-frequency gap for sovereign spread with a genuinely primary central-bank feed and a clean JSON API. Cross-source triangulation vs World Bank GEM raises trust.
sources.terms: BCRP open statistics; attribute Banco Central de Reserva del Perú (source: JPMorgan). Link-out per figure.

sources.name: WorldGovernmentBonds — Mexico 5Y CDS + live yield curve
sources.whatItGives: Mexico 5-year sovereign CDS spread and implied default probability, plus a full live government-bond yield curve (3M→30Y) with day/week changes.
sources.accessMethod: Stable scrape (structured HTML tables)
sources.whyItMatters: CDS is the market's direct price of default risk and has no free official Mexican source; this is the standard free proxy. Curve page also cross-checks the Banxico-derived curve. Scrape-fragile — wrap with a fail-closed check.
sources.terms: Third-party aggregator (not primary) — use as convenience/cross-check, link-out; verify their attribution before treating as citable. Prefer Banxico for the domestic curve and cite WGB only for CDS.

sources.name: S&P/BMV IPC (Mexbol) equity index — level & return
sources.whatItGives: The benchmark Mexican equity index (35 large-caps): level, daily return, and history — the headline 'how are Mexican stocks doing' number.
sources.accessMethod: Official: S&P DJI (licensed) / BMV (entitlement-gated API). Free practical feed: Yahoo Finance ^MXX chart JSON or CSV download.
sources.auth: none for Yahoo endpoint; official S&P/BMV real-time is paid/licensed
sources.cadence: daily (delayed on free feed; EOD is safe)
sources.whyItMatters: Equities are the one major market surface the current wiring lacks. Watch the honesty bar: the free Yahoo ^MXX feed is not primary — cite S&P/BMV as the index authority and label the level 'delayed/indicative,' or use EOD only. A real decision: license S&P DJI vs. free-feed-with-caveat.
sources.terms: Yahoo feed is unofficial, personal-use gray area — do not present as official; link-out to S&P/BMV as the authoritative index. Consider EOD-only to stay clean.

notes: Hunted for official/primary, free, zero-dependency-Node-wireable sources on Mexican population, projections, and migration corridors (both directions). Prioritized primary government APIs (INEGI, CONAPO, SEGOB-UPM) and US-side data that moves Mexico (CBP encounters, US Census ACS/IDB). Duplicate flags: Banxico SIE (already wired) covers the *aggregate* remittance dollar total; the KNOMAD Bilateral Matrix below is NOT a duplicate — it adds the US-origin corridor breakdown and migrant-stock allocation. Data Mexico (already wired, OLAP) carries some INEGI census/employment cubes, so the INEGI BISE indicator API partially overlaps for headline census counts but is broader (municipal, historical series, ENADID-derived indicators) — flagged as partial-duplicate. Everything below was verified reachable via WebSearch/WebFetch. Two caveats worth noting for the wiring pass: (1) CBP publishes as CSV files linked off cbp.gov plus a Socrata-backed Public Data Portal — the stable connector target is the dated CSV; the Socrata JSON endpoint requires resolving the dataset ID off the portal. (2) SEGOB/UPM and CONAPO publish as downloadable XLSX/CSV bundles (no REST API) — connectors should target the stable file URLs and parse. (3) cbp.gov returned 403 to automated fetch (bot filtering) — a connector will likely need a browser-like User-Agent header; the CSV file URLs themselves are stable and public.

sources.name: INEGI Banco de Indicadores API (BISE/BIE)
sources.whatItGives: Programmatic access to INEGI's full indicator catalog: total population and Censo 2020 counts, age/sex structure, intercensal estimates, and ENADID-derived demographic indicators, at national/state/municipal geography with full historical series.
keyIndicators: Population by five-year age group and sex
sources.accessMethod: REST API
sources.auth: free token (register on INEGI dev portal)
sources.cadence: Census decennial; intercensal ~5yr; most indicator series updated as INEGI releases; API itself always-on
sources.whyItMatters: The authoritative primary source for who lives in Mexico and where — the demographic spine every migration figure is normalized against. Clean JSON, free token, national-to-municipal.
sources.terms: INEGI open data terms — free reuse incl. commercial with attribution

sources.name: CONAPO Proyecciones de Población 2020–2070 + Conciliación Demográfica 1950–2019
sources.whatItGives: Mexico's official population projections to 2070 (and reconciled historical series 1950–2019), by single year of age, sex, and state — including births, deaths, and net international migration assumptions baked into each projection.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download
sources.cadence: Reconciled/re-based irregularly (major revision 2023); mid-year estimates annual
sources.whyItMatters: The only official forward-looking view of Mexico's population and its embedded migration assumptions — essential for any 'where is Mexico headed' framing. Bulk CSV/XLSX, no API, stable file URLs.
sources.terms: Libre Uso MX open data license — reuse with attribution

sources.name: US CBP Southwest Land Border Encounters
sources.whatItGives: Monthly counts of migrant encounters (Title 8 Border Patrol apprehensions, OFO inadmissibles) at the US southwest border, broken down by citizenship, sector, demographic (single adult / family / unaccompanied minor), and month — the headline northbound-flow indicator.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download
sources.endpoint: https://www.cbp.gov/newsroom/stats/southwest-land-border-encounters (dated CSV downloads) + Socrata-backed CBP Public Data Portal https://www.cbp.gov/newsroom/stats/cbp-public-data-portal (JSON via /api/v3/views/{id}/query.json once dataset ID resolved)
sources.auth: none (Socrata app token optional for higher rate limits)
sources.whyItMatters: The single most-watched, politically loaded number in the US–Mexico migration relationship; directly feeds border/tariff/politics narratives. CSV is stable; note cbp.gov bot-filters automated fetches (needs UA header).
sources.terms: US government work — public domain

sources.name: DHS Office of Homeland Security Statistics (OHSS) — CBP Encounters
sources.whatItGives: DHS's cleaned, cross-component encounter dataset (CBP + interior), an analytic layer over raw CBP counts with consistent definitions over time — a more research-grade version of the same border-flow signal.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download
sources.endpoint: https://ohss.dhs.gov/khsm/cbp-encounters (downloadable data tables under the Key Homeland Security Metrics hub)
sources.whyItMatters: Provides a definitional cross-check on raw CBP numbers and handles the recidivism/double-count problem that raw encounter data doesn't — useful for an honesty-first brief that wants the caveated figure.
sources.terms: US government work — public domain

sources.name: US Census Bureau American Community Survey (ACS) API
sources.whatItGives: Annual estimates of the Mexican-born population living in the US — the stock on the other side of the corridor — by US state, year of entry, citizenship status, and socioeconomic characteristics.
keyIndicators: Mexican immigrants by state of residence
sources.accessMethod: REST API
sources.cadence: Annual (1-year ACS released each Sept; 5-year each Dec)
sources.whyItMatters: Quantifies the ~10–11M Mexican diaspora in the US that drives remittances and the migration corridor — the destination-side counterpart to CONAPO. Clean JSON REST, free key.
sources.terms: US government work — public domain

sources.name: US Census Bureau International Database (IDB) API
sources.whatItGives: US Census's independent demographic model of Mexico: population by single year of age and sex, fertility, mortality, and net migration, from base year through 2100 — an external second opinion to CONAPO.
sources.accessMethod: REST API
sources.cadence: Re-based roughly annually / on major releases
sources.whyItMatters: Lets the brief show two independent projections of Mexico's demographic future (CONAPO vs US Census) — exactly the kind of sourced, side-by-side honesty that differentiates. Same free Census key as ACS.
sources.terms: US government work — public domain

sources.name: World Bank KNOMAD Bilateral Remittance Matrix
sources.whatItGives: Estimated remittance flows disaggregated by source and destination country — specifically the US→Mexico corridor and Mexico's other inbound corridors — allocated via migrant-stock and income methodology.
keyIndicators: Mexico inbound remittances by source country
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download
sources.endpoint: https://www.knomad.org/data/remittances (bilateral matrix XLSX, e.g. .../bilateral_remittance_matrix_2021_0.xlsx) + programmatic via World Bank Data360 https://data360.worldbank.org/en/indicator/WB_KNOMAD_BRE
sources.whyItMatters: Banxico (already wired) gives the aggregate remittance dollar total; this adds the corridor breakdown — how much comes specifically from the US and how it maps to migrant stock. Not a duplicate; complements Banxico.
sources.terms: World Bank open data — CC-BY 4.0

sources.name: SEGOB Unidad de Política Migratoria (UPM) — Boletines Estadísticos
sources.whatItGives: Mexico's official migration-event statistics: repatriations/returns of Mexican nationals from the US (by state, reception point, age, sex) and irregular transit migrants presented/returned by Mexican authorities (by nationality).
keyIndicators: Irregular migrants presented to Mexican authorities
Migrants by reception point / border state
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download
sources.endpoint: https://portales.segob.gob.mx/es/PoliticaMigratoria/Boletines_Estadisticos (annual XLSX/CSV bulletin bundles; see also CuadrosBOLETIN?Anual={year}&Secc={n})
sources.whyItMatters: The primary Mexican-government view of both the northbound-return flow AND Mexico-as-transit-country — a dimension no US source captures. Downloadable XLSX, stable URL pattern by year.
sources.terms: Libre Uso MX / Mexican government open data — reuse with attribution

sources.name: INEGI ENADID 2023 (Encuesta Nacional de la Dinámica Demográfica)
sources.whatItGives: Mexico's flagship demographic-dynamics survey measuring internal migration (state-to-state moves) and international migration (household members who left/returned), plus fertility and mortality — the primary source on internal migration corridors.
keyIndicators: Internal migration by state of origin/destination
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download
sources.endpoint: https://www.inegi.org.mx/programas/enadid/2023/ (microdata + tabulados; catalog: https://www.inegi.org.mx/rnm/index.php/catalog/981). Headline indicators also queryable via the INEGI BISE API.
sources.auth: none (bulk microdata); free token if pulled via BISE API
sources.whyItMatters: The only official read on internal migration corridors inside Mexico (which states drain, which absorb) — directly serves Alan's migration-corridor interest beyond the US border. Headline indicators wireable via BISE; deep cuts via microdata.
sources.terms: INEGI open data terms — reuse with attribution

sources.name: Instituto de los Mexicanos en el Exterior (IME) — Matrícula Consular statistics
sources.whatItGives: Statistics from High-Security Consular Registrations issued by Mexican consulates: the Mexican diaspora in the US profiled by state of origin, US state of residence, sex, age, and education — a corridor map of where Mexican migrants come from and settle.
keyIndicators: Matrículas issued by US state of residence
Migrants by Mexican state of origin
sources.accessMethod: scrape
sources.whyItMatters: Uniquely maps origin-state → US-destination corridors from the Mexican consular side (e.g. Michoacán→California) — granular corridor detail unavailable elsewhere. Published as HTML tables, so needs a stable scrape.
sources.terms: Mexican government open data — reuse with attribution

sources.name: Migration Policy Institute (MPI) Migration Data Hub
sources.whatItGives: Curated, analysis-ready tables on Mexican immigrants in the US (stock over time, net migration, unauthorized estimates) and bilateral flows — a cleaned secondary layer over Census/DHS sources with 'GET THE DATA' downloads.
keyIndicators: Mexican-born population in US over time
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download
sources.cadence: Updated as underlying ACS/DHS data releases (annual+)
sources.whyItMatters: Fills the gap Census leaves — unauthorized-population estimates and consistent long time series — with careful methodology. Secondary source (flag as such), per-chart downloads rather than a clean API, so lower wireability.
sources.terms: MPI content — free to cite with attribution; not fully open-licensed, so link-out/cite rather than republish

domain: News feeds (Mexican & US) + official release calendars — POINT/link-out only, never republish
notes: Scope: this run hunted the NEWS + CALENDARS domain only (per directive), going beyond the already-wired GDELT DOC 2.0. Two sub-buckets: (A) named trusted-press feeds for a POINT/link-out news module (headline + dek + URL + timestamp only — honesty law: never store/republish article body; many of these outlets are paywalled/copyrighted, so link-out is both legal and required), and (B) official release CALENDARS to power a "What-to-Watch" module (know the exact date/time each official series drops). Verification: RSS URLs were confirmed via search/aggregator listings and outlet RSS index pages; a couple of exact feed paths (El Financiero, Milenio) could not be pinned to a canonical XML URL and are flagged medium/hard — validate the endpoint before wiring. Highest-value NEW technical find = SIDOF (Diario Oficial de la Federación) official JSON API: getJSON endpoints for the daily gazette, documents, indicators (incl. official FX/UDIS) and notes — primary, dated, structured, free. Recommended wiring pattern: one generic RSS connector (RSS 2.0/Atom) fed a config table of feed URLs, so adding an outlet = one row, not new code; plus a dedicated SIDOF-JSON connector and a lightweight calendar connector that parses the INEGI/Banxico calendar pages/PDFs into upcoming-release events. De-dup: none of these duplicate Banxico SIE / Data Mexico / World Bank (those are data series); they complement GDELT by giving named, allowlist-controlled sources rather than GDELT's aggregate. Anti-scope note: avoid third-party RSS-generator services (rss.app, feedspot, feeder.co) in production — they add a dependency and a ToS/reliability risk; use them only to discover the outlet's own native feed, then wire the outlet directly.

sources.name: El Universal — RSS (native feeds)

sources.accessMethod: RSS
sources.terms: Headlines link-out only; do not republish article body. Content copyrighted — POINT/link-out satisfies honesty law.
sources.whyItMatters: Broadest mainstream Mexican daily; anchors the general-news lane of the POINT module. Confirm the exact live feed path (legacy vs current CMS) at wire time.

sources.accessMethod: RSS

sources.whyItMatters: Business/finance angle on Mexico from a major outlet; complements macro data with narrative on companies and markets.

JSON + PDF. This is the primary source for what's legally in force + official daily indicators.
sources.accessMethod: REST/JSON download
sources.endpoint: https://sidof.segob.gob.mx/datos_abiertos (JSON: https://sidof.segob.gob.mx/datos_abiertos/getJSON/65 gazette, /getJSON/61 indicators, /getJSON/43 documents, /getJSON/57 notes; status https://sidof.segob.gob.mx/apiStatus)

Primary and fully citable.

sources.name: gob.mx / SEGOB press — official government news
sources.whatItGives: Official federal government press releases and news (Presidencia + secretarías). Central 'Noticias México' hub plus per-secretaría prensa sections; some agencies expose RSS (e.g. STPS).
sources.accessMethod: RSS where available / scrape
