sources.name: INEGI — Calendario de Difusión (release calendar / What-to-Watch)
sources.whatItGives: Official schedule of every INEGI statistical release (INPC/inflation, PIB/GDP, IGAE, employment ENOE, IOAE, etc.) with exact publication dates; semiannual calendar + live 'Sala de prensa' calendar page. Also an Indicadores API for the actual data.
sources.accessMethod: Scrape of calendar page + PDF; data via Indicadores API
sources.endpoint: Calendar: https://www.inegi.org.mx/app/saladeprensa/calendario/ ; PDF (2026): https://www.inegi.org.mx/contenidos/saladeprensa/doc/cal_2026.pdf ; Data API: https://www.inegi.org.mx/servicios/api_indicadores.html (JSON-stat, free token)
sources.auth: none for calendar; free token for Indicadores API
sources.terms: Official/public, fully citable. Parse the calendar page/PDF into upcoming-release events.
sources.whyItMatters: Powers the 'What-to-Watch' module — tells outsiders exactly WHEN the next inflation/GDP/jobs print lands so the brief can pre-stage. Note: the Indicadores data API partially overlaps series already covered via Banxico SIE/World Bank, but the CALENDAR itself is new and non-duplicative.

sources.name: Banxico — Calendario de Difusión / press-release schedule (What-to-Watch)
sources.whatItGives: Official schedule of Banxico publications and press releases: the 8 monetary-policy decision dates (Thursdays 13:00 CST), minutes (2 weeks later), inflation reports, and other releases. Calendar viewer + PDF.
sources.accessMethod: Scrape of JSP calendar viewer + PDF
sources.endpoint: ES: https://www.banxico.org.mx/viewers2/JSP/calendarioDifusion_es.jsp ; EN: https://www.banxico.org.mx/viewers/JSP/calendarioDifusion_en.jsp?static=y ; 2026 decisions PDF linked from https://www.banxico.org.mx/publicaciones-y-prensa/anuncios-de-las-decisiones-de-politica-monetaria/anuncios-politica-monetaria-t.html
sources.terms: Official/public, citable. Complements (does not duplicate) the already-wired Banxico SIE data API — SIE is the rate value; this is the SCHEDULE of when decisions/reports drop.
sources.whyItMatters: Rate-decision dates are the single most-watched Mexico calendar events for markets; pairing decision dates with the SIE policy-rate series lets the brief count down to and then immediately reflect each decision.

sources.name: datos.gob.mx — CKAN open-data API (SHCP fiscal + cross-agency)
sources.whatItGives: National open-data portal on CKAN: programmatic package/dataset search + resource downloads. SHCP publishes ~48 datasets incl. Estadísticas Oportunas de Finanzas Públicas (fiscal indicators, debt, revenue/spending).
sources.accessMethod: CKAN REST API (JSON) + CSV/JSON resource downloads
sources.terms: Open data (mostly Libre Uso MX license) — citable, reusable with attribution. Verify each dataset's resource freshness (some datos.gob.mx resources lag the agency's own site).
sources.whyItMatters: Fills the FISCAL gap — SHCP public-finance data (deficit, debt, oil revenue) isn't in Banxico SIE or Data Mexico. CKAN gives a uniform API across many agencies, so it's a reusable connector, not a one-off. Confirm SHCP's own site vs portal for the freshest fiscal print.

domain: Cost of living, livability & competitiveness (relocator + PE personas)
notes: Hunt focused on official/primary + free first, then usable commercial. Duplicate flags: (1) INEGI Banco de Indicadores API overlaps Banxico SIE ONLY for national CPI/inflation headline — but it adds subnational INPC-by-city, wages, ENSU/ENVIPE series, well-being, so it is a major net-new source, not a dup. (2) ENVIPE is NOT a dup of SESNSP: SESNSP = administrative homicide counts; ENVIPE = household victimization + PERCEIVED insecurity + peso cost of crime. (3) World Bank REST is wired for NATIONAL structural indicators; Subnational Doing Business is state-level and a different product (but discontinued — flagged). (4) Data México (wired) is built on INEGI microdata for trade/employment/complexity; INEGI DENUE (establishment directory) is establishment-level and largely non-overlapping. Wireability reality: the INEGI Indicadores REST API is the single highest-leverage add — one free token unlocks dozens of subnational livability/price/security/wage series in JSON, schedulable. Numbeo is the best single cross-city cost-of-living+crime+healthcare API but is paid and crowdsourced (label clearly, not "official"). IMD and Mercer are authoritative but paywalled with no clean feed — treat as annual manual/point sources, not connectors. Real-estate rent gap: no official national rent index exists; SHF (sale prices, official, CSV) is the citable spine; live rent asking-prices only exist behind portal scrapes (Inmuebles24/Propiedades.com) which are ToS-risky — omitted from wire list.

sources.name: INEGI Banco de Indicadores API (BISE/BIE)
sources.whatItGives: Single REST gateway to thousands of INEGI indicator series, many at state and municipality level: subnational INPC (inflation by 55 price cities), average wages/remuneraciones, employment, and published aggregates of ENSU (security perception), ENVIPE, ENOE. The backbone for almost every cost-of-living and livability series below.
keyIndicators: INPC / inflation by city (55 metro areas)
Average salary & real remunerations by state
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON/JSONP/XML)
sources.whyItMatters: The highest-leverage single wire in this domain: one free token yields subnational, dated, primary series for prices, wages, and security across all 32 states — exactly the relocator/PE granularity Banxico's national feed lacks.
sources.terms: Open public data; INEGI terms allow reuse with attribution. Cite series ID + INEGI.

sources.name: IMCO Índice de Competitividad Estatal (ICE)
sources.whatItGives: The definitive Mexican state business-climate/competitiveness index: 32 states scored 0–100 across 53 indicators in 6 subindices (rule of law, government, society, environment, factor markets, innovation/economy). Rankings + per-indicator drill-down.
keyIndicators: Overall competitiveness rank & score by state
sources.accessMethod: CSV/Excel + dynamic consultation tool download (annual dataset)
sources.whyItMatters: The single most-cited state competitiveness benchmark in Mexico — the PE/site-selection persona's anchor. Composite plus components lets the Brief show why a state ranks where it does.
sources.terms: Published for public use with attribution to IMCO; not an API — schedule an annual fetch/parse of the posted dataset file.

sources.name: SHF Índice de Precios de la Vivienda
sources.whatItGives: Official quarterly house-price index for Mexico (mortgage-appraisal based), national + by metro area + housing type, with downloadable historical CSVs. The citable spine for housing cost / affordability.
keyIndicators: Index by metro area (CDMX, GDL, MTY, Tijuana, Querétaro…)
sources.accessMethod: CSV download (open data)
sources.whyItMatters: The only official, dated, primary housing-price series in Mexico — essential cost-of-living + investment signal, and clean to wire (static CSV refreshed on a schedule).
sources.terms: Gob.mx open data, free reuse with attribution to Sociedad Hipotecaria Federal.

sources.name: INEGI ENSU — Encuesta Nacional de Seguridad Pública Urbana
sources.whatItGives: Quarterly perceived-security survey for ~90 urban centers: share of adults who feel unsafe in their city, by city and over time, plus perception of authorities and specific-location fear. The livability 'do I feel safe here' layer that homicide counts miss.
keyIndicators: % adults feeling unsafe in their city (by city)
Fear at ATM / public transport / streets
sources.accessMethod: REST API (published aggregates via INEGI Indicadores) + CSV open data / microdata
sources.auth: none (open data) / free token if via Indicadores API
sources.whyItMatters: Perceived safety by city is a top relocator decision input and is not captured by SESNSP homicides (already wired) — complementary, not duplicative.
sources.terms: INEGI open data, attribution.

sources.name: INEGI ENVIPE — victimization & cost of insecurity
sources.whatItGives: Annual national victimization survey: household victimization rates, dark-figure (unreported crime), perceived insecurity, and the peso cost of crime to households (national + by state). Distinct from SESNSP administrative counts.
keyIndicators: Economic cost of crime (pesos, % of GDP)
sources.accessMethod: CSV/microdata download + press-release tabulados
sources.whyItMatters: Puts a dated peso figure on insecurity per state — a livability AND business-cost signal PE cares about — and covers unreported crime that SESNSP structurally misses.
sources.terms: INEGI open data / microdata, free with attribution.

sources.name: INEGI ENOE — income & labor by state
sources.whatItGives: Quarterly labor-force survey with wages/income, informality, and unemployment for all 32 states — the income side of cost-of-living affordability and the labor-availability side of business climate.
keyIndicators: Average monthly labor income by state
sources.accessMethod: Tabulados (CSV/XLS) + published series via INEGI Indicadores API
sources.whyItMatters: Local wages contextualize cost of living (affordability = prices vs income) and signal labor cost/availability for PE — at state granularity Data México's aggregates don't foreground.
sources.terms: INEGI open data, attribution. Partial overlap with Data México employment (different cut: income focus, state tabulados).

sources.name: INEGI Precios Promedio (average product prices by city)
sources.whatItGives: Average observed prices for specific INPC goods/services (tortilla, gasoline, groceries, services) across the 46–55 INPC price cities — a concrete, itemized cost-of-living basket, not just an index.
keyIndicators: Average price per product per city (pesos)
sources.accessMethod: Web app (parameterized query / scrape); underlying series also in Indicadores API
sources.whyItMatters: Lets the Brief show real peso prices of a basket by city — the most tangible relocator cost-of-living view — from an official source (vs crowdsourced Numbeo/Expatistan).
sources.terms: INEGI open data; note INEGI caveat that city-level figures are 'informational' (sample representative at national level). Cite accordingly.

sources.name: OECD Regional Well-Being
sources.whatItGives: 11 well-being dimensions (income, jobs, housing, health, safety, environment, education, access to services, civic engagement, community, life satisfaction) scored 0–10 for all 32 Mexican states, comparable across 400+ OECD regions.
sources.accessMethod: Excel download + OECD Data Explorer (SDMX REST API)
sources.cadence: updated ~annually (data monitor refreshes at least quarterly)
sources.whyItMatters: Gives Mexican states an internationally comparable, methodologically consistent livability scorecard — lets the outsider persona benchmark a Mexican state against places they already know.
sources.terms: OECD open terms (attribution). SDMX API is fully machine-readable.

sources.name: Numbeo Data API (cost of living, crime, healthcare)
sources.whatItGives: Cross-city crowdsourced indices and itemized prices with wide Mexican city coverage: cost-of-living index, rent index, crime index, safety index, healthcare index, pollution/traffic — all queryable per city or country via clean JSON.
keyIndicators: Cost of living + rent index by city
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON)
sources.auth: paid (API key; commercial subscription, limited free/dev tier)
sources.whyItMatters: The single best cross-city API for one-call cost-of-living + crime + healthcare comparisons, with far more Mexican cities than official sources expose in a comparable format.
sources.cost: paid subscription (tiered by call volume); small free dev quota
sources.terms: Commercial API license required; crowdsourced — label as perception/community data, NOT official. Attribution to Numbeo required.

sources.name: Expatistan cost of living
sources.whatItGives: Crowdsourced cost-of-living comparison across 52 items for major Mexican cities (CDMX, Monterrey, Guadalajara…), with city-vs-city and city-vs-foreign-city comparisons the relocator persona reasons in.
sources.accessMethod: Stable HTML scrape (no official API)
sources.whyItMatters: Fast 'is City A cheaper than the city I live in now' framing for outsiders; useful cross-check against Numbeo. Lower priority — crowdsourced and scrape-only.
sources.terms: No public API; scraping is ToS-gray — respect robots/rate limits, cite Expatistan, treat as community data. Secondary given honesty-law bar.

sources.name: INEGI DENUE — Directorio Estadístico Nacional de Unidades Económicas
sources.whatItGives: Geolocated directory of ~5M active business establishments by sector, size, and exact location — a business-density/agglomeration and site-selection layer for the PE/investor persona, queryable by radius and area.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON)
sources.whyItMatters: Lets the Brief characterize a state/city's actual business fabric (who operates there, how dense) for competitiveness/site-selection — establishment-level detail Data México's aggregates don't provide.
sources.terms: INEGI open data, free token, attribution. Largely non-overlapping with wired Data México.

sources.name: World Bank Subnational Business Ready / Doing Business — Mexico
sources.whatItGives: State- and city-level regulatory business-climate scores for Mexican states (starting a business, construction permits, property registration, contract enforcement) — the classic subnational red-tape benchmark.
sources.accessMethod: Report + dataset download (Excel/PDF); no live API
sources.cadence: irregular / discontinued — legacy Doing Business retired; B-READY successor rolling out
sources.whyItMatters: Authoritative subnational regulatory benchmark for the PE persona, but flagged: Doing Business was discontinued and B-READY subnational Mexico coverage is not yet a scheduled feed. Use as a dated point-in-time source, not a live wire.
sources.terms: World Bank open data/CC-BY; stale — mark data vintage explicitly. Distinct from wired World Bank national REST (subnational, different product).

sources.name: IMD World Competitiveness Ranking (national)
sources.whatItGives: Mexico's national competitiveness rank among ~70 economies with sub-pillar detail (economic performance, government efficiency, business efficiency, infrastructure) and multi-year time series.
sources.accessMethod: Paywalled online database + annual report (no free API)
sources.whyItMatters: Globally recognized competitiveness benchmark for the PE/investor macro view, but paywalled with no clean feed — treat as an annual manually-entered point figure with link-out, not a connector. Lower priority vs free IMCO for subnational.
sources.terms: Proprietary; headline rank citable with attribution, full data licensed. Do not ingest licensed dataset.

sources.name: Mercer Cost of Living & Quality of Living City Rankings
sources.whatItGives: Global city rankings for cost of living (expat basket) and quality of living, including Mexican cities (CDMX, Monterrey) — the HR/relocation-industry standard for assignee cost and livability.
sources.accessMethod: Published rankings (web/PDF) + paid detailed reports; no free API
sources.auth: paid for detail; headline ranks public
sources.whyItMatters: Authoritative expat-oriented cost/quality benchmark for the relocator persona, but only headline ranks are free and there is no feed — capture as an annual point figure with link-out. Lower priority.
sources.terms: Proprietary; cite headline rank with attribution to Mercer, link-out only. No republishing of licensed detail.

domain: International benchmarks & complexity (comparative context for Mexico vs peers)
notes: Focus: multilateral/official comparators that let Mexico be benchmarked against peer countries (Vietnam, Poland, Brazil, Turkey, etc.) on macro, trade, complexity, tariffs, and productivity. All are official or authoritative-academic, free, and reachable from a zero-dependency Node connector. Duplicate flags: World Bank REST and Harvard Atlas/Growth Lab are already wired — the entries below marked duplicatesExisting=true either extend them with new endpoints (Data360 consolidates 40x more series incl. IMF/UNCTAD/WEF passthrough; Atlas GraphQL + Dataverse bulk are the deeper endpoints the Economy room flagged) or overlap partially. IMF, OECD, WTO, UN Comtrade, OEC.world, WITS/TRAINS, UNCTADstat, Penn World Table, and Our World in Data are NEW and non-duplicative. Two practical wiring notes: (1) SDMX-JSON sources (IMF new portal, OECD, UNCTAD) are verbose — the IMF DataMapper flat-JSON API and OWID grapher CSV are far cheaper connectors for the same benchmarking numbers and should be preferred where they cover the indicator; (2) OEC.world latest-year data and subnational need a paid token, but historical country-country-HS flows and all product/complexity metadata are free unauthenticated. Peer-country national statistics offices (Vietnam GSO, Poland GUS, Brazil IBGE, Turkey TUIK) were considered but belong to a country-specific domain rather than this comparative-benchmark hunt; the multilateral sources here already carry all peers on a harmonized basis, which is what benchmarking needs.

sources.name: IMF DataMapper API (WEO passthrough)
sources.whatItGives: Flat-JSON time series for every WEO indicator across all countries and analytical groups — the easiest way to pull comparable macro numbers for Mexico vs any peer set in one call. Backed by the World Economic Outlook database (national accounts, inflation, unemployment, fiscal balance, current account, gross debt, per-capita GDP PPP).
sources.accessMethod: REST API (flat JSON)
sources.cadence: WEO refreshed twice/year (April & October); interim revisions
sources.whyItMatters: The single cleanest peer-benchmark feed: one indicator ID + a list of ISO3 codes returns Mexico beside Vietnam/Poland/Brazil on a harmonized basis, no SDMX parsing. Directly fills the 'how does Mexico compare' gap for the Economy room.
sources.terms: IMF data, free reuse with attribution; cite IMF World Economic Outlook

sources.name: IMF Data — SDMX 2.1/3.0 REST (WEO, IFS, BOP, GFS, DOT)
sources.whatItGives: Full-resolution official IMF databases via SDMX: International Financial Statistics (IFS), Balance of Payments (BOP), Government Finance Statistics (GFS), Direction of Trade Statistics (DOT — bilateral trade incl. Mexico–US–China), plus WEO. Deeper and higher-frequency (monthly/quarterly) than DataMapper.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (SDMX-JSON / SDMX-ML)
sources.endpoint: Legacy: http://dataservices.imf.org/REST/SDMX_JSON.svc/CompactData/{DB}/{key}  (e.g. IFS, DOT). New portal + SDMX 3.0: https://data.imf.org/en/Resource-Pages/IMF-API
sources.auth: none (new portal may issue free keys for high volume)
sources.cadence: IFS/DOT monthly–quarterly; WEO biannual; GFS annual
sources.whyItMatters: DOT gives dated bilateral trade to benchmark Mexico's US-dependence vs Vietnam's/Poland's; IFS gives comparable monetary/reserve series. SDMX is verbose but stable and fully citable to primary.
sources.terms: IMF terms, free with attribution; note the legacy dataservices endpoint is being migrated to the new data.imf.org SDMX 3.0 service — wire the new one and keep legacy as fallback

sources.name: OECD Data API (SDMX-JSON)
sources.whatItGives: OECD's full statistical catalogue over standard SDMX REST: leading indicators (CLI/MEI), quarterly GDP, PPPs, productivity (GDP per hour), unit labour costs, FDI, PISA, tax revenue, trade in value-added (TiVA). Mexico is an OECD member, so it sits natively alongside OECD peers and key partners.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (SDMX-JSON, also CSV/XML)
sources.cadence: Varies: CLI/MEI monthly, GDP quarterly, productivity annual
sources.whyItMatters: Only OECD gives comparable productivity and unit-labour-cost series — the 'is Mexico's nearshoring competitive' benchmark vs Poland/Turkey. CLI is an early-warning turning-point signal for the Mexican economy.
sources.terms: OECD terms of use, free reuse with attribution; some third-party series restricted (flagged in metadata)

sources.name: World Bank Data360 API
sources.whatItGives: Consolidated World Bank Group platform exposing ~40x more series than the old Indicators API — including WDI plus passthrough of IMF, UNCTAD, WEF, WGI (governance), Doing-Business-successor and other providers, all under one REST API with harmonized metadata. Search + time-series endpoints.
keyIndicators: Worldwide Governance Indicators (rule of law, control of corruption)
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON)
sources.whyItMatters: One connector reaches dozens of comparative indicators (governance, logistics, human capital) for Mexico vs peers without wiring each provider separately — a force-multiplier for the 'more sources' differentiator.
sources.terms: CC-BY 4.0 for World Bank-originated data; some third-party indicators carry provider-specific redistribution limits noted in metadata — respect per-indicator terms

sources.name: OEC.world API (Observatory of Economic Complexity)
sources.whatItGives: Trade flows and economic-complexity metrics at HS product level by country and destination — bilateral exports/imports, Product Complexity Index (PCI), Economic Complexity Index (ECI), and the product space / opportunity outlook. This is the explicit gap flagged on the Economy room (product & destination detail at HS level).
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON via Tesseract OLAP)
sources.auth: free token (public/historical & all metadata unauthenticated; Pro/Premium token needed for latest-year, subnational, higher volume)
sources.cadence: Annual (2024 trade landing ~April–June 2026), aligned to Comtrade/HS releases
sources.cost: free tier; paid Pro/Premium for latest & subnational
sources.whyItMatters: Directly fills the flagged Economy-room gap: lets the brief show what Mexico exports, to whom, at HS granularity, and rank its complexity vs Vietnam/Poland — the core 'complexity' story. Note: run by Datawheel, same OLAP tech as the already-wired Data Mexico, so the connector pattern is familiar.
sources.terms: Data attribution to OEC; verify redistribution scope for paid-tier latest data before republishing figures

sources.name: Harvard Growth Lab — Atlas GraphQL API + Dataverse bulk
sources.whatItGives: The deeper Atlas endpoints behind the already-wired Growth Lab: a public GraphQL API for on-demand complexity/trade queries, plus full cleaned bilateral trade datasets (HS & SITC) and computed ECI/PCI/proximity as bulk files on Harvard Dataverse. The methodology gold-standard for complexity rankings.
sources.accessMethod: GraphQL API + CSV/DTA bulk download (Dataverse)
sources.endpoint: GraphQL docs: https://github.com/harvard-growth-lab/api-docs/blob/main/atlas.md  ; downloads: https://atlas.hks.harvard.edu/data-downloads/ ; bulk DOI dataset: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataverse/atlas
sources.whyItMatters: Extends what's already wired: the GraphQL API supports live comparative queries and the Dataverse files let us pre-bake Mexico-vs-peer complexity tables offline. Independent cross-check on OEC.world's numbers.
sources.terms: Freely available public resource, per-file citation required; cite Atlas of Economic Complexity / Harvard Growth Lab

sources.name: UN Comtrade API (v1 public/preview)
sources.whatItGives: The authoritative raw bilateral merchandise trade database (reporter × partner × HS commodity × flow) that Mexico's own trade story and every complexity dataset ultimately derive from. Free v1 tier for annual/monthly flows; primary source for benchmarking trade structure directly.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON)
sources.auth: free token (register on developer portal for higher limits; preview endpoint works unauthenticated with tight caps)
sources.whyItMatters: Primary, most-citable trade source — lets the brief link any trade figure one tap from the UN original, and benchmark Mexico's trade composition against peers month-by-month rather than waiting for annual complexity releases.
sources.terms: UN Comtrade attribution; free-tier rate/volume limits — cache and schedule pulls

sources.name: WTO Timeseries / Stats API
sources.whatItGives: WTO's flagship API for merchandise and services trade (annual/quarterly/monthly) and — critically — market-access indicators: bound, MFN-applied and preferential tariffs, plus non-tariff information. The tariff angle is the current US–Mexico story.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (JSON) + CSV/Excel export
sources.endpoint: https://apiportal.wto.org/  (portal with API console & keys; data platforms: https://timeseries.wto.org/ and https://ttd.wto.org/en)
sources.whyItMatters: The tariff/market-access dimension no other wired source covers well — lets the brief benchmark the tariff wall Mexico faces vs what Vietnam/Poland face, straight from the WTO authority, dated and citable.
sources.terms: WTO terms, free reuse with attribution

sources.name: World Bank WITS API (UNCTAD TRAINS + WTO IDB)
sources.whatItGives: World Integrated Trade Solution: consolidated tariff (UNCTAD TRAINS MFN & preferential, WTO IDB/CTS bound) and trade data at HS6, by country, in one REST API — the practical way to pull comparable effective tariff schedules and trade indicators for Mexico and peers.
sources.accessMethod: REST API (SDMX/XML) + bulk download
sources.whyItMatters: Complements WTO Timeseries with product-level (HS6) tariff detail and combines TRAINS+IDB in one call — the granular tariff-benchmark layer for the trade/tariffs story. Fully free and unauthenticated.
sources.terms: World Bank / UNCTAD attribution, free reuse

sources.name: UNCTADstat Data Centre
sources.whatItGives: UNCTAD's own statistics on trade, FDI, maritime/logistics, commodities, and development — including productive-capacities and non-tariff-measure indicators not in the World Bank set. Bulk CSV per table plus a data-centre viewer; SDMX access via UN infrastructure.
keyIndicators: Merchandise trade indices & terms of trade
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON bulk download + SDMX
sources.endpoint: https://unctadstat.unctad.org/datacentre/dataviewer/  (bulk files per dataset; UN SDMX/UNdata service: http://data.un.org/Host.aspx?Content=API)
sources.whyItMatters: Liner Shipping Connectivity and terms-of-trade are unique benchmarks for Mexico's export-logistics competitiveness vs Vietnam; FDI series cross-check the nearshoring narrative. Distinct coverage from anything wired.
sources.terms: UNCTAD attribution, free reuse; scrape/download is of published statistical tables (no copyrighted text)

sources.name: Penn World Table 11.0 (Groningen GGDC)
sources.whatItGives: Academic gold-standard panel of comparable real income, output, input and productivity across ~185 countries, 1950–2023 — real GDP at chained PPPs, capital stock, TFP, labour share, human capital index. The rigorous long-run productivity/level benchmark.
sources.accessMethod: CSV / Excel / Stata bulk download (also interactive query export)
sources.endpoint: https://www.rug.nl/ggdc/productivity/pwt/  (data files via DataverseNL, linked from that page)
sources.cadence: Major release every ~3–4 years (11.0 covers to 2023)
sources.whyItMatters: Lets the brief benchmark Mexico's TFP and income level against peers on a methodologically consistent long-run basis — the deep 'why has Mexico converged or not' comparison that annual macro feeds can't support. Static file, trivial to bake.
sources.terms: CC-BY 4.0; cite Feenstra, Inklaar & Timmer (2015), PWT

sources.name: Our World in Data — Grapher Chart API
sources.whatItGives: Direct CSV + JSON-metadata URLs for thousands of curated charts, each aggregating primary sources (World Bank, UN, OWID-processed) into ready comparable cross-country series. The cheapest possible connector for many benchmark indicators, with provenance metadata baked in.
sources.accessMethod: CSV/JSON download (stable per-chart URLs)
sources.whyItMatters: A pragmatic fast-path: one predictable URL per indicator returns Mexico-vs-peer data plus a machine-readable source citation, satisfying the honesty law (dated, one-tap-to-source) with almost no connector code. Good backfill where SDMX is overkill.
sources.terms: OWID content CC-BY; underlying source terms pass through in metadata — cite both OWID and the original source shown in metadata.json
